cd /home/claude && python3 << 'EOF'
import openpyxl
from openpyxl.styles import Font, PatternFill, Alignment, Border, Side

# Create a new workbook
wb = openpyxl.Workbook()
ws = wb.active
ws.title = "Academic Record"

# Set up headers
headers = ["Course Name", "Credit Hours", "Semester"]
ws.append(headers)

# Style the header row
header_fill = PatternFill(start_color="4472C4", end_color="4472C4", fill_type="solid")
header_font = Font(bold=True, color="FFFFFF", size=11)

for cell in ws[1]:
    cell.fill = header_fill
    cell.font = header_font
    cell.alignment = Alignment(horizontal="center", vertical="center")

# Data from the transcript
courses_data = [
    # Fall-2022
    ("Computer Programming", 3, "Fall-2022"),
    ("Applied Calculus & Analytical Geometry", 3, "Fall-2022"),
    ("Computing Fundamentals", 2, "Fall-2022"),
    ("Functional English", 3, "Fall-2022"),
    ("Applied Physics", 2, "Fall-2022"),
    ("Computer Programming Lab", 1, "Fall-2022"),
    ("Applied Physics Lab", 1, "Fall-2022"),
    ("Computing Fundamentals Lab", 1, "Fall-2022"),
    
    # Spring-2023
    ("Linear Algebra", 3, "Spring-2023"),
    ("Occupational Health and Safety", 1, "Spring-2023"),
    ("Object Oriented Programming Lab", 1, "Spring-2023"),
    ("Object Oriented Programming", 3, "Spring-2023"),
    ("Islamic Studies/Ethics", 2, "Spring-2023"),
    ("Introduction to Software Engineering", 3, "Spring-2023"),
    ("Discrete Structures", 3, "Spring-2023"),
    ("Communication Skills", 2, "Spring-2023"),
    
    # Fall-2023
    ("Data Structures & Algorithms", 3, "Fall-2023"),
    ("Data Structures & Algorithms Lab", 1, "Fall-2023"),
    ("Software Requirement Engineering", 3, "Fall-2023"),
    ("Probability & Statistics", 3, "Fall-2023"),
    ("Computer Architecture and Logic Design Lab", 1, "Fall-2023"),
    ("Computer Architecture and Logic Design", 3, "Fall-2023"),
    ("Pakistan Studies and Global Perspective", 2, "Fall-2023"),
    ("Introduction to Media Studies", 2, "Fall-2023"),
    
    # Spring-2024
    ("Engineering Management", 3, "Spring-2024"),
    ("Software Design & Architecture Lab", 1, "Spring-2024"),
    ("Software Design & Architecture", 2, "Spring-2024"),
    ("Database Management Systems Lab", 1, "Spring-2024"),
    ("Database Management Systems", 3, "Spring-2024"),
    ("Design and Analysis of Algorithms", 3, "Spring-2024"),
    ("Operating Systems Lab", 1, "Spring-2024"),
    ("Operating Systems", 3, "Spring-2024"),
    
    # Fall-2024
    ("Computer Communication & Networks", 3, "Fall-2024"),
    ("Computer Communication & Networks Lab", 1, "Fall-2024"),
    ("Formal Methods in Software Engineering", 3, "Fall-2024"),
    ("Software Construction", 2, "Fall-2024"),
    ("Software Construction Lab", 1, "Fall-2024"),
    ("Introduction to Data Science", 2, "Fall-2024"),
    ("Introduction to Data Science Lab", 1, "Fall-2024"),
    ("Embedded System Design", 2, "Fall-2024"),
    ("Embedded System Design Lab", 1, "Fall-2024"),
    ("Accounting and Finance", 2, "Fall-2024"),
    
    # Spring-2025
    ("Software Quality Engineering", 3, "Spring-2025"),
    ("Human Computer Interaction", 3, "Spring-2025"),
    ("Technical Writing & Presentation Skills", 3, "Spring-2025"),
    ("Cloud Computing", 2, "Spring-2025"),
    ("Cloud Computing Lab", 1, "Spring-2025"),
    ("Artificial Intelligence", 2, "Spring-2025"),
    ("Artificial Intelligence Lab", 1, "Spring-2025"),
    ("Software Applications for Mobile Devices", 2, "Spring-2025"),
    ("Software Applications for Mobile Devices Lab", 1, "Spring-2025"),
    
    # Fall-2025
    ("Numerical Analysis", 2, "Fall-2025"),
    ("Software Project Management", 3, "Fall-2025"),
    ("Project I", 3, "Fall-2025"),
    ("Information Security", 3, "Fall-2025"),
    ("Numerical Analysis Lab", 1, "Fall-2025"),
    ("DevOps", 3, "Fall-2025"),
    ("Entrepreneurship", 2, "Fall-2025"),
    
    # Spring-2026
    ("Operations Research", 3, "Spring-2026"),
    ("Natural Language Processing", 3, "Spring-2026"),
    ("Agent Based Computing", 3, "Spring-2026"),
    ("Project II", 3, "Spring-2026"),
]

# Add data rows
for course_name, credit_hours, semester in courses_data:
    ws.append([course_name, credit_hours, semester])

# Style data cells
thin_border = Border(
    left=Side(style='thin'),
    right=Side(style='thin'),
    top=Side(style='thin'),
    bottom=Side(style='thin')
)

for row in ws.iter_rows(min_row=1, max_row=ws.max_row, min_col=1, max_col=3):
    for cell in row:
        cell.border = thin_border
        if cell.row > 1:  # Data rows
            cell.alignment = Alignment(horizontal="left", vertical="center")

# Adjust column widths
ws.column_dimensions['A'].width = 45
ws.column_dimensions['B'].width = 15
ws.column_dimensions['C'].width = 15

# Save the workbook
wb.save('/mnt/user-data/outputs/academic_record.xlsx')
print("Excel file created successfully!")

EOF

display_content.text: Reading the docx skill to understand how to create a properly formatted Word document

content.text:
1	---
     2	name: docx
     3	description: "Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation."
     4	license: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms
     5	---
     6	
     7	# DOCX creation, editing, and analysis
     8	
     9	## Overview
    10	
    11	A .docx file is a ZIP archive containing XML files.
    12	
    13	## Quick Reference
    14	
    15	| Task | Approach |
    16	|------|----------|
    17	| Read/analyze content | `extract-text`, or unpack for raw XML |
    18	| Create new document | Use `docx-js` - see Creating New Documents below |
    19	| Edit existing document | Unpack → edit XML → repack - see Editing Existing Documents below |
    20	
    21	### Converting .doc to .docx
    22	
    23	Legacy `.doc` files must be converted before editing:
    24	
    25	```bash
    26	python scripts/office/soffice.py --headless --convert-to docx document.doc
    27	```
    28	
    29	### Reading Content
    30	
    31	```bash
    32	# Text extraction as markdown
    33	extract-text document.docx
    34	
    35	# Show tracked changes instead of accepting them
    36	pandoc --track-changes=all document.docx -o output.md
    37	
    38	# Raw XML access
    39	python scripts/office/unpack.py document.docx unpacked/
    40	```
    41	
    42	### Converting to Images
    43	
    44	```bash
    45	python scripts/office/soffice.py --headless --convert-to pdf document.docx
    46	pdftoppm -jpeg -r 150 document.pdf page
    47	```
    48	
    49	### Accepting Tracked Changes
    50	
    51	To produce a clean document with all tracked changes accepted (requires LibreOffice):
    52	
    53	```bash
    54	python scripts/accept_changes.py input.docx output.docx
    55	```
    56	
    57	---
    58	
    59	## Creating New Documents
    60	
    61	Generate .docx files with JavaScript, then validate. Install: `npm install -g docx`
    62	
    63	### Setup
    64	```javascript
    65	const { Document, Packer, Paragraph, TextRun, Table, TableRow, TableCell, ImageRun,
    66	        Header, Footer, AlignmentType, PageOrientation, LevelFormat, ExternalHyperlink,
    67	        InternalHyperlink, Bookmark, FootnoteReferenceRun, PositionalTab,
    68	        PositionalTabAlignment, PositionalTabRelativeTo, PositionalTabLeader,
    69	        TabStopType, TabStopPosition, Column, SectionType,
    70	        TableOfContents, HeadingLevel, BorderStyle, WidthType, ShadingType,
    71	        VerticalAlign, PageNumber, PageBreak } = require('docx');
    72	
    73	const doc = new Document({ sections: [{ children: [/* content */] }] });
    74	Packer.toBuffer(doc).then(buffer => fs.writeFileSync("doc.docx", buffer));
    75	```
    76	
    77	### Validation
    78	After creating the file, validate it. If validation fails, unpack, fix the XML, and repack.
    79	```bash
    80	python scripts/office/validate.py doc.docx
    81	```
    82	
    83	### Page Size
    84	
    85	```javascript
    86	// CRITICAL: docx-js defaults to A4, not US Letter
    87	// Always set page size explicitly for consistent results
    88	sections: [{
    89	  properties: {
    90	    page: {
    91	      size: {
    92	        width: 12240,   // 8.5 inches in DXA
    93	        height: 15840   // 11 inches in DXA
    94	      },
    95	      margin: { top: 1440, right: 1440, bottom: 1440, left: 1440 } // 1 inch margins
    96	    }
    97	  },
    98	  children: [/* content */]
    99	}]
   100	```
   101	
   102	**Common page sizes (DXA units, 1440 DXA = 1 inch):**
   103	
   104	| Paper | Width | Height | Content Width (1" margins) |
   105	|-------|-------|--------|---------------------------|
   106	| US Letter | 12,240 | 15,840 | 9,360 |
   107	| A4 (default) | 11,906 | 16,838 | 9,026 |
   108	
   109	**Landscape orientation:** docx-js swaps width/height internally, so pass portrait dimensions and let it handle the swap:
   110	```javascript
   111	size: {
   112	  width: 12240,   // Pass SHORT edge as width
   113	  height: 15840,  // Pass LONG edge as height
   114	  orientation: PageOrientation.LANDSCAPE  // docx-js swaps them in the XML
   115	},
   116	// Content width = 15840 - left margin - right margin (uses the long edge)
   117	```
   118	
   119	### Styles (Override Built-in Headings)
   120	
   121	Use Arial as the default font (universally supported). Keep titles black for readability.
   122	
   123	```javascript
   124	const doc = new Document({
   125	  styles: {
   126	    default: { document: { run: { font: "Arial", size: 24 } } }, // 12pt default
   127	    paragraphStyles: [
   128	      // IMPORTANT: Use exact IDs to override built-in styles
   129	      { id: "Heading1", name: "Heading 1", basedOn: "Normal", next: "Normal", quickFormat: true,
   130	        run: { size: 32, bold: true, font: "Arial" },
   131	        paragraph: { spacing: { before: 240, after: 240 }, outlineLevel: 0 } }, // outlineLevel required for TOC
   132	      { id: "Heading2", name: "Heading 2", basedOn: "Normal", next: "Normal", quickFormat: true,
   133	        run: { size: 28, bold: true, font: "Arial" },
   134	        paragraph: { spacing: { before: 180, after: 180 }, outlineLevel: 1 } },
   135	    ]
   136	  },
   137	  sections: [{
   138	    children: [
   139	      new Paragraph({ heading: HeadingLevel.HEADING_1, children: [new TextRun("Title")] }),
   140	    ]
   141	  }]
   142	});
   143	```
   144	
   145	### Lists (NEVER use unicode bullets)
   146	
   147	```javascript
   148	// ❌ WRONG - never manually insert bullet characters
   149	new Paragraph({ children: [new TextRun("• Item")] })  // BAD
   150	new Paragraph({ children: [new TextRun("\u2022 Item")] })  // BAD
   151	
   152	// ✅ CORRECT - use numbering config with LevelFormat.BULLET
   153	const doc = new Document({
   154	  numbering: {
   155	    config: [
   156	      { reference: "bullets",
   157	        levels: [{ level: 0, format: LevelFormat.BULLET, text: "•", alignment: AlignmentType.LEFT,
   158	          style: { paragraph: { indent: { left: 720, hanging: 360 } } } }] },
   159	      { reference: "numbers",
   160	        levels: [{ level: 0, format: LevelFormat.DECIMAL, text: "%1.", alignment: AlignmentType.LEFT,
   161	          style: { paragraph: { indent: { left: 720, hanging: 360 } } } }] },
   162	    ]
   163	  },
   164	  sections: [{
   165	    children: [
   166	      new Paragraph({ numbering: { reference: "bullets", level: 0 },
   167	        children: [new TextRun("Bullet item")] }),
   168	      new Paragraph({ numbering: { reference: "numbers", level: 0 },
   169	        children: [new TextRun("Numbered item")] }),
   170	    ]
   171	  }]
   172	});
   173	
   174	// ⚠️ Each reference creates INDEPENDENT numbering
   175	// Same reference = continues (1,2,3 then 4,5,6)
   176	// Different reference = restarts (1,2,3 then 1,2,3)
   177	```
   178	
   179	### Tables
   180	
   181	**CRITICAL: Tables need dual widths** - set both `columnWidths` on the table AND `width` on each cell. Without both, tables render incorrectly on some platforms.
   182	
   183	```javascript
   184	// CRITICAL: Always set table width for consistent rendering
   185	// CRITICAL: Use ShadingType.CLEAR (not SOLID) to prevent black backgrounds
   186	const border = { style: BorderStyle.SINGLE, size: 1, color: "CCCCCC" };
   187	const borders = { top: border, bottom: border, left: border, right: border };
   188	
   189	new Table({
   190	  width: { size: 9360, type: WidthType.DXA }, // Always use DXA (percentages break in Google Docs)
   191	  columnWidths: [4680, 4680], // Must sum to table width (DXA: 1440 = 1 inch)
   192	  rows: [
   193	    new TableRow({
   194	      children: [
   195	        new TableCell({
   196	          borders,
   197	          width: { size: 4680, type: WidthType.DXA }, // Also set on each cell
   198	          shading: { fill: "D5E8F0", type: ShadingType.CLEAR }, // CLEAR not SOLID
   199	          margins: { top: 80, bottom: 80, left: 120, right: 120 }, // Cell padding (internal, not added to width)
   200	          children: [new Paragraph({ children: [new TextRun("Cell")] })]
   201	        })
   202	      ]
   203	    })
   204	  ]
   205	})
   206	```
   207	
   208	**Table width calculation:**
   209	
   210	Always use `WidthType.DXA` — `WidthType.PERCENTAGE` breaks in Google Docs.
   211	
   212	```javascript
   213	// Table width = sum of columnWidths = content width
   214	// US Letter with 1" margins: 12240 - 2880 = 9360 DXA
   215	width: { size: 9360, type: WidthType.DXA },
	< truncated lines 216-378 >
   379	```
   380	
   381	### Critical Rules for docx-js
   382	
   383	- **Set page size explicitly** - docx-js defaults to A4; use US Letter (12240 x 15840 DXA) for US documents
   384	- **Landscape: pass portrait dimensions** - docx-js swaps width/height internally; pass short edge as `width`, long edge as `height`, and set `orientation: PageOrientation.LANDSCAPE`
   385	- **Never use `\n`** - use separate Paragraph elements
   386	- **Never use unicode bullets** - use `LevelFormat.BULLET` with numbering config
   387	- **PageBreak must be in Paragraph** - standalone creates invalid XML
   388	- **ImageRun requires `type`** - always specify png/jpg/etc
   389	- **Always set table `width` with DXA** - never use `WidthType.PERCENTAGE` (breaks in Google Docs)
   390	- **Tables need dual widths** - `columnWidths` array AND cell `width`, both must match
   391	- **Table width = sum of columnWidths** - for DXA, ensure they add up exactly
   392	- **Always add cell margins** - use `margins: { top: 80, bottom: 80, left: 120, right: 120 }` for readable padding
   393	- **Use `ShadingType.CLEAR`** - never SOLID for table shading
   394	- **Never use tables as dividers/rules** - cells have minimum height and render as empty boxes (including in headers/footers); use `border: { bottom: { style: BorderStyle.SINGLE, size: 6, color: "2E75B6", space: 1 } }` on a Paragraph instead. For two-column footers, use tab stops (see Tab Stops section), not tables
   395	- **TOC requires HeadingLevel only** - no custom styles on heading paragraphs
   396	- **Override built-in styles** - use exact IDs: "Heading1", "Heading2", etc.
   397	- **Include `outlineLevel`** - required for TOC (0 for H1, 1 for H2, etc.)
   398	
   399	---
   400	
   401	## Editing Existing Documents
   402	
   403	**Follow all 3 steps in order.**
   404	
   405	### Step 1: Unpack
   406	```bash
   407	python scripts/office/unpack.py document.docx unpacked/
   408	```
   409	Extracts XML, pretty-prints, merges adjacent runs, and converts smart quotes to XML entities (`“` etc.) so they survive editing. Use `--merge-runs false` to skip run merging.
   410	
   411	### Step 2: Edit XML
   412	
   413	Edit files in `unpacked/word/`. See XML Reference below for patterns.
   414	
   415	**Use "Claude" as the author** for tracked changes and comments, unless the user explicitly requests use of a different name.
   416	
   417	**Use the Edit tool directly for string replacement. Do not write Python scripts.** Scripts introduce unnecessary complexity. The Edit tool shows exactly what is being replaced.
   418	
   419	**CRITICAL: Use smart quotes for new content.** When adding text with apostrophes or quotes, use XML entities to produce smart quotes:
   420	```xml
   421	<!-- Use these entities for professional typography -->
   422	<w:t>Here’s a quote: “Hello”</w:t>
   423	```
   424	| Entity | Character |
   425	|--------|-----------|
   426	| `‘` | ‘ (left single) |
   427	| `’` | ’ (right single / apostrophe) |
   428	| `“` | “ (left double) |
   429	| `”` | ” (right double) |
   430	
   431	**Adding comments:** Use `comment.py` to handle boilerplate across multiple XML files (text must be pre-escaped XML):
   432	```bash
   433	python scripts/comment.py unpacked/ 0 "Comment text with & and ’"
   434	python scripts/comment.py unpacked/ 1 "Reply text" --parent 0  # reply to comment 0
   435	python scripts/comment.py unpacked/ 0 "Text" --author "Custom Author"  # custom author name
   436	```
   437	Then add markers to document.xml (see Comments in XML Reference).
   438	
   439	### Step 3: Pack
   440	```bash
   441	python scripts/office/pack.py unpacked/ output.docx --original document.docx
   442	```
   443	Validates with auto-repair, condenses XML, and creates DOCX. Use `--validate false` to skip.
   444	
   445	**Auto-repair will fix:**
   446	- `durableId` >= 0x7FFFFFFF (regenerates valid ID)
   447	- Missing `xml:space="preserve"` on `<w:t>` with whitespace
   448	
   449	**Auto-repair won't fix:**
   450	- Malformed XML, invalid element nesting, missing relationships, schema violations
   451	
   452	### Common Pitfalls
   453	
   454	- **Replace entire `<w:r>` elements**: When adding tracked changes, replace the whole `<w:r>...</w:r>` block with `<w:del>...<w:ins>...` as siblings. Don't inject tracked change tags inside a run.
   455	- **Preserve `<w:rPr>` formatting**: Copy the original run's `<w:rPr>` block into your tracked change runs to maintain bold, font size, etc.
   456	
   457	---
   458	
   459	## XML Reference
   460	
   461	### Schema Compliance
   462	
   463	- **Element order in `<w:pPr>`**: `<w:pStyle>`, `<w:numPr>`, `<w:spacing>`, `<w:ind>`, `<w:jc>`, `<w:rPr>` last
   464	- **Whitespace**: Add `xml:space="preserve"` to `<w:t>` with leading/trailing spaces
   465	- **RSIDs**: Must be 8-digit hex (e.g., `00AB1234`)
   466	
   467	### Tracked Changes
   468	
   469	**Insertion:**
   470	```xml
   471	<w:ins w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   472	  <w:r><w:t>inserted text</w:t></w:r>
   473	</w:ins>
   474	```
   475	
   476	**Deletion:**
   477	```xml
   478	<w:del w:id="2" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   479	  <w:r><w:delText>deleted text</w:delText></w:r>
   480	</w:del>
   481	```
   482	
   483	**Inside `<w:del>`**: Use `<w:delText>` instead of `<w:t>`, and `<w:delInstrText>` instead of `<w:instrText>`.
   484	
   485	**Minimal edits** - only mark what changes:
   486	```xml
   487	<!-- Change "30 days" to "60 days" -->
   488	<w:r><w:t>The term is </w:t></w:r>
   489	<w:del w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="...">
   490	  <w:r><w:delText>30</w:delText></w:r>
   491	</w:del>
   492	<w:ins w:id="2" w:author="Claude" w:date="...">
   493	  <w:r><w:t>60</w:t></w:r>
   494	</w:ins>
   495	<w:r><w:t> days.</w:t></w:r>
   496	```
   497	
   498	**Deleting entire paragraphs/list items** - when removing ALL content from a paragraph, also mark the paragraph mark as deleted so it merges with the next paragraph. Add `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>`:
   499	```xml
   500	<w:p>
   501	  <w:pPr>
   502	    <w:numPr>...</w:numPr>  <!-- list numbering if present -->
   503	    <w:rPr>
   504	      <w:del w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z"/>
   505	    </w:rPr>
   506	  </w:pPr>
   507	  <w:del w:id="2" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   508	    <w:r><w:delText>Entire paragraph content being deleted...</w:delText></w:r>
   509	  </w:del>
   510	</w:p>
   511	```
   512	Without the `<w:del/>` in `<w:pPr><w:rPr>`, accepting changes leaves an empty paragraph/list item.
   513	
   514	**Rejecting another author's insertion** - nest deletion inside their insertion:
   515	```xml
   516	<w:ins w:author="Jane" w:id="5">
   517	  <w:del w:author="Claude" w:id="10">
   518	    <w:r><w:delText>their inserted text</w:delText></w:r>
   519	  </w:del>
   520	</w:ins>
   521	```
   522	
   523	**Restoring another author's deletion** - add insertion after (don't modify their deletion):
   524	```xml
   525	<w:del w:author="Jane" w:id="5">
   526	  <w:r><w:delText>deleted text</w:delText></w:r>
   527	</w:del>
   528	<w:ins w:author="Claude" w:id="10">
   529	  <w:r><w:t>deleted text</w:t></w:r>
   530	</w:ins>
   531	```
   532	
   533	### Comments
   534	
   535	After running `comment.py` (see Step 2), add markers to document.xml. For replies, use `--parent` flag and nest markers inside the parent's.
   536	
   537	**CRITICAL: `<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` are siblings of `<w:r>`, never inside `<w:r>`.**
   538	
   539	```xml
   540	<!-- Comment markers are direct children of w:p, never inside w:r -->
   541	<w:commentRangeStart w:id="0"/>
   542	<w:del w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   543	  <w:r><w:delText>deleted</w:delText></w:r>
   544	</w:del>
   545	<w:r><w:t> more text</w:t></w:r>
   546	<w:commentRangeEnd w:id="0"/>
   547	<w:r><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="CommentReference"/></w:rPr><w:commentReference w:id="0"/></w:r>
   548	
   549	<!-- Comment 0 with reply 1 nested inside -->
   550	<w:commentRangeStart w:id="0"/>
   551	  <w:commentRangeStart w:id="1"/>
   552	  <w:r><w:t>text</w:t></w:r>
   553	  <w:commentRangeEnd w:id="1"/>
   554	<w:commentRangeEnd w:id="0"/>
   555	<w:r><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="CommentReference"/></w:rPr><w:commentReference w:id="0"/></w:r>
   556	<w:r><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="CommentReference"/></w:rPr><w:commentReference w:id="1"/></w:r>
   557	```
   558	
   559	### Images
   560	
   561	1. Add image file to `word/media/`
   562	2. Add relationship to `word/_rels/document.xml.rels`:
   563	```xml
   564	<Relationship Id="rId5" Type=".../image" Target="media/image1.png"/>
   565	```
   566	3. Add content type to `[Content_Types].xml`:
   567	```xml
   568	<Default Extension="png" ContentType="image/png"/>
   569	```
   570	4. Reference in document.xml:
   571	```xml
   572	<w:drawing>
   573	  <wp:inline>
   574	    <wp:extent cx="914400" cy="914400"/>  <!-- EMUs: 914400 = 1 inch -->
   575	    <a:graphic>
   576	      <a:graphicData uri=".../picture">
   577	        <pic:pic>
   578	          <pic:blipFill><a:blip r:embed="rId5"/></pic:blipFill>
   579	        </pic:pic>
   580	      </a:graphicData>
   581	    </a:graphic>
   582	  </wp:inline>
   583	</w:drawing>
   584	```
   585	
   586	---
   587	
   588	## Dependencies
   589	
   590	- **pandoc**: Text extraction
   591	- **docx**: `npm install -g docx` (new documents)
   592	- **LibreOffice**: PDF conversion (auto-configured for sandboxed environments via `scripts/office/soffice.py`)
   593	- **Poppler**: `pdftoppm` for images
   594
Now I'll create a condensed one-page resume with all content preserved, streamlined skills section, and better spacing.

content.text:
1	---
     2	name: docx
     3	description: "Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation."
     4	license: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms
     5	---
     6	
     7	# DOCX creation, editing, and analysis
     8	
     9	## Overview
    10	
    11	A .docx file is a ZIP archive containing XML files.
    12	
    13	## Quick Reference
    14	
    15	| Task | Approach |
    16	|------|----------|
    17	| Read/analyze content | `extract-text`, or unpack for raw XML |
    18	| Create new document | Use `docx-js` - see Creating New Documents below |
    19	| Edit existing document | Unpack → edit XML → repack - see Editing Existing Documents below |
    20	
    21	### Converting .doc to .docx
    22	
    23	Legacy `.doc` files must be converted before editing:
    24	
    25	```bash
    26	python scripts/office/soffice.py --headless --convert-to docx document.doc
    27	```
    28	
    29	### Reading Content
    30	
    31	```bash
    32	# Text extraction as markdown
    33	extract-text document.docx
    34	
    35	# Show tracked changes instead of accepting them
    36	pandoc --track-changes=all document.docx -o output.md
    37	
    38	# Raw XML access
    39	python scripts/office/unpack.py document.docx unpacked/
    40	```
    41	
    42	### Converting to Images
    43	
    44	```bash
    45	python scripts/office/soffice.py --headless --convert-to pdf document.docx
    46	pdftoppm -jpeg -r 150 document.pdf page
    47	```
    48	
    49	### Accepting Tracked Changes
    50	
    51	To produce a clean document with all tracked changes accepted (requires LibreOffice):
    52	
    53	```bash
    54	python scripts/accept_changes.py input.docx output.docx
    55	```
    56	
    57	---
    58	
    59	## Creating New Documents
    60	
    61	Generate .docx files with JavaScript, then validate. Install: `npm install -g docx`
    62	
    63	### Setup
    64	```javascript
    65	const { Document, Packer, Paragraph, TextRun, Table, TableRow, TableCell, ImageRun,
    66	        Header, Footer, AlignmentType, PageOrientation, LevelFormat, ExternalHyperlink,
    67	        InternalHyperlink, Bookmark, FootnoteReferenceRun, PositionalTab,
    68	        PositionalTabAlignment, PositionalTabRelativeTo, PositionalTabLeader,
    69	        TabStopType, TabStopPosition, Column, SectionType,
    70	        TableOfContents, HeadingLevel, BorderStyle, WidthType, ShadingType,
    71	        VerticalAlign, PageNumber, PageBreak } = require('docx');
    72	
    73	const doc = new Document({ sections: [{ children: [/* content */] }] });
    74	Packer.toBuffer(doc).then(buffer => fs.writeFileSync("doc.docx", buffer));
    75	```
    76	
    77	### Validation
    78	After creating the file, validate it. If validation fails, unpack, fix the XML, and repack.
    79	```bash
    80	python scripts/office/validate.py doc.docx
    81	```
    82	
    83	### Page Size
    84	
    85	```javascript
    86	// CRITICAL: docx-js defaults to A4, not US Letter
    87	// Always set page size explicitly for consistent results
    88	sections: [{
    89	  properties: {
    90	    page: {
    91	      size: {
    92	        width: 12240,   // 8.5 inches in DXA
    93	        height: 15840   // 11 inches in DXA
    94	      },
    95	      margin: { top: 1440, right: 1440, bottom: 1440, left: 1440 } // 1 inch margins
    96	    }
    97	  },
    98	  children: [/* content */]
    99	}]
   100	```
   101	
   102	**Common page sizes (DXA units, 1440 DXA = 1 inch):**
   103	
   104	| Paper | Width | Height | Content Width (1" margins) |
   105	|-------|-------|--------|---------------------------|
   106	| US Letter | 12,240 | 15,840 | 9,360 |
   107	| A4 (default) | 11,906 | 16,838 | 9,026 |
   108	
   109	**Landscape orientation:** docx-js swaps width/height internally, so pass portrait dimensions and let it handle the swap:
   110	```javascript
   111	size: {
   112	  width: 12240,   // Pass SHORT edge as width
   113	  height: 15840,  // Pass LONG edge as height
   114	  orientation: PageOrientation.LANDSCAPE  // docx-js swaps them in the XML
   115	},
   116	// Content width = 15840 - left margin - right margin (uses the long edge)
   117	```
   118	
   119	### Styles (Override Built-in Headings)
   120	
   121	Use Arial as the default font (universally supported). Keep titles black for readability.
   122	
   123	```javascript
   124	const doc = new Document({
   125	  styles: {
   126	    default: { document: { run: { font: "Arial", size: 24 } } }, // 12pt default
   127	    paragraphStyles: [
   128	      // IMPORTANT: Use exact IDs to override built-in styles
   129	      { id: "Heading1", name: "Heading 1", basedOn: "Normal", next: "Normal", quickFormat: true,
   130	        run: { size: 32, bold: true, font: "Arial" },
   131	        paragraph: { spacing: { before: 240, after: 240 }, outlineLevel: 0 } }, // outlineLevel required for TOC
   132	      { id: "Heading2", name: "Heading 2", basedOn: "Normal", next: "Normal", quickFormat: true,
   133	        run: { size: 28, bold: true, font: "Arial" },
   134	        paragraph: { spacing: { before: 180, after: 180 }, outlineLevel: 1 } },
   135	    ]
   136	  },
   137	  sections: [{
   138	    children: [
   139	      new Paragraph({ heading: HeadingLevel.HEADING_1, children: [new TextRun("Title")] }),
   140	    ]
   141	  }]
   142	});
   143	```
   144	
   145	### Lists (NEVER use unicode bullets)
   146	
   147	```javascript
   148	// ❌ WRONG - never manually insert bullet characters
   149	new Paragraph({ children: [new TextRun("• Item")] })  // BAD
   150	new Paragraph({ children: [new TextRun("\u2022 Item")] })  // BAD
   151	
   152	// ✅ CORRECT - use numbering config with LevelFormat.BULLET
   153	const doc = new Document({
   154	  numbering: {
   155	    config: [
   156	      { reference: "bullets",
   157	        levels: [{ level: 0, format: LevelFormat.BULLET, text: "•", alignment: AlignmentType.LEFT,
   158	          style: { paragraph: { indent: { left: 720, hanging: 360 } } } }] },
   159	      { reference: "numbers",
   160	        levels: [{ level: 0, format: LevelFormat.DECIMAL, text: "%1.", alignment: AlignmentType.LEFT,
   161	          style: { paragraph: { indent: { left: 720, hanging: 360 } } } }] },
   162	    ]
   163	  },
   164	  sections: [{
   165	    children: [
   166	      new Paragraph({ numbering: { reference: "bullets", level: 0 },
   167	        children: [new TextRun("Bullet item")] }),
   168	      new Paragraph({ numbering: { reference: "numbers", level: 0 },
   169	        children: [new TextRun("Numbered item")] }),
   170	    ]
   171	  }]
   172	});
   173	
   174	// ⚠️ Each reference creates INDEPENDENT numbering
   175	// Same reference = continues (1,2,3 then 4,5,6)
   176	// Different reference = restarts (1,2,3 then 1,2,3)
   177	```
   178	
   179	### Tables
   180	
   181	**CRITICAL: Tables need dual widths** - set both `columnWidths` on the table AND `width` on each cell. Without both, tables render incorrectly on some platforms.
   182	
   183	```javascript
   184	// CRITICAL: Always set table width for consistent rendering
   185	// CRITICAL: Use ShadingType.CLEAR (not SOLID) to prevent black backgrounds
   186	const border = { style: BorderStyle.SINGLE, size: 1, color: "CCCCCC" };
   187	const borders = { top: border, bottom: border, left: border, right: border };
   188	
   189	new Table({
   190	  width: { size: 9360, type: WidthType.DXA }, // Always use DXA (percentages break in Google Docs)
   191	  columnWidths: [4680, 4680], // Must sum to table width (DXA: 1440 = 1 inch)
   192	  rows: [
   193	    new TableRow({
   194	      children: [
   195	        new TableCell({
   196	          borders,
   197	          width: { size: 4680, type: WidthType.DXA }, // Also set on each cell
   198	          shading: { fill: "D5E8F0", type: ShadingType.CLEAR }, // CLEAR not SOLID
   199	          margins: { top: 80, bottom: 80, left: 120, right: 120 }, // Cell padding (internal, not added to width)
   200	          children: [new Paragraph({ children: [new TextRun("Cell")] })]
   201	        })
   202	      ]
   203	    })
   204	  ]
   205	})
   206	```
   207	
   208	**Table width calculation:**
   209	
   210	Always use `WidthType.DXA` — `WidthType.PERCENTAGE` breaks in Google Docs.
   211	
   212	```javascript
   213	// Table width = sum of columnWidths = content width
   214	// US Letter with 1" margins: 12240 - 2880 = 9360 DXA
   215	width: { size: 9360, type: WidthType.DXA },
	< truncated lines 216-378 >
   379	```
   380	
   381	### Critical Rules for docx-js
   382	
   383	- **Set page size explicitly** - docx-js defaults to A4; use US Letter (12240 x 15840 DXA) for US documents
   384	- **Landscape: pass portrait dimensions** - docx-js swaps width/height internally; pass short edge as `width`, long edge as `height`, and set `orientation: PageOrientation.LANDSCAPE`
   385	- **Never use `\n`** - use separate Paragraph elements
   386	- **Never use unicode bullets** - use `LevelFormat.BULLET` with numbering config
   387	- **PageBreak must be in Paragraph** - standalone creates invalid XML
   388	- **ImageRun requires `type`** - always specify png/jpg/etc
   389	- **Always set table `width` with DXA** - never use `WidthType.PERCENTAGE` (breaks in Google Docs)
   390	- **Tables need dual widths** - `columnWidths` array AND cell `width`, both must match
   391	- **Table width = sum of columnWidths** - for DXA, ensure they add up exactly
   392	- **Always add cell margins** - use `margins: { top: 80, bottom: 80, left: 120, right: 120 }` for readable padding
   393	- **Use `ShadingType.CLEAR`** - never SOLID for table shading
   394	- **Never use tables as dividers/rules** - cells have minimum height and render as empty boxes (including in headers/footers); use `border: { bottom: { style: BorderStyle.SINGLE, size: 6, color: "2E75B6", space: 1 } }` on a Paragraph instead. For two-column footers, use tab stops (see Tab Stops section), not tables
   395	- **TOC requires HeadingLevel only** - no custom styles on heading paragraphs
   396	- **Override built-in styles** - use exact IDs: "Heading1", "Heading2", etc.
   397	- **Include `outlineLevel`** - required for TOC (0 for H1, 1 for H2, etc.)
   398	
   399	---
   400	
   401	## Editing Existing Documents
   402	
   403	**Follow all 3 steps in order.**
   404	
   405	### Step 1: Unpack
   406	```bash
   407	python scripts/office/unpack.py document.docx unpacked/
   408	```
   409	Extracts XML, pretty-prints, merges adjacent runs, and converts smart quotes to XML entities (`“` etc.) so they survive editing. Use `--merge-runs false` to skip run merging.
   410	
   411	### Step 2: Edit XML
   412	
   413	Edit files in `unpacked/word/`. See XML Reference below for patterns.
   414	
   415	**Use "Claude" as the author** for tracked changes and comments, unless the user explicitly requests use of a different name.
   416	
   417	**Use the Edit tool directly for string replacement. Do not write Python scripts.** Scripts introduce unnecessary complexity. The Edit tool shows exactly what is being replaced.
   418	
   419	**CRITICAL: Use smart quotes for new content.** When adding text with apostrophes or quotes, use XML entities to produce smart quotes:
   420	```xml
   421	<!-- Use these entities for professional typography -->
   422	<w:t>Here’s a quote: “Hello”</w:t>
   423	```
   424	| Entity | Character |
   425	|--------|-----------|
   426	| `‘` | ‘ (left single) |
   427	| `’` | ’ (right single / apostrophe) |
   428	| `“` | “ (left double) |
   429	| `”` | ” (right double) |
   430	
   431	**Adding comments:** Use `comment.py` to handle boilerplate across multiple XML files (text must be pre-escaped XML):
   432	```bash
   433	python scripts/comment.py unpacked/ 0 "Comment text with & and ’"
   434	python scripts/comment.py unpacked/ 1 "Reply text" --parent 0  # reply to comment 0
   435	python scripts/comment.py unpacked/ 0 "Text" --author "Custom Author"  # custom author name
   436	```
   437	Then add markers to document.xml (see Comments in XML Reference).
   438	
   439	### Step 3: Pack
   440	```bash
   441	python scripts/office/pack.py unpacked/ output.docx --original document.docx
   442	```
   443	Validates with auto-repair, condenses XML, and creates DOCX. Use `--validate false` to skip.
   444	
   445	**Auto-repair will fix:**
   446	- `durableId` >= 0x7FFFFFFF (regenerates valid ID)
   447	- Missing `xml:space="preserve"` on `<w:t>` with whitespace
   448	
   449	**Auto-repair won't fix:**
   450	- Malformed XML, invalid element nesting, missing relationships, schema violations
   451	
   452	### Common Pitfalls
   453	
   454	- **Replace entire `<w:r>` elements**: When adding tracked changes, replace the whole `<w:r>...</w:r>` block with `<w:del>...<w:ins>...` as siblings. Don't inject tracked change tags inside a run.
   455	- **Preserve `<w:rPr>` formatting**: Copy the original run's `<w:rPr>` block into your tracked change runs to maintain bold, font size, etc.
   456	
   457	---
   458	
   459	## XML Reference
   460	
   461	### Schema Compliance
   462	
   463	- **Element order in `<w:pPr>`**: `<w:pStyle>`, `<w:numPr>`, `<w:spacing>`, `<w:ind>`, `<w:jc>`, `<w:rPr>` last
   464	- **Whitespace**: Add `xml:space="preserve"` to `<w:t>` with leading/trailing spaces
   465	- **RSIDs**: Must be 8-digit hex (e.g., `00AB1234`)
   466	
   467	### Tracked Changes
   468	
   469	**Insertion:**
   470	```xml
   471	<w:ins w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   472	  <w:r><w:t>inserted text</w:t></w:r>
   473	</w:ins>
   474	```
   475	
   476	**Deletion:**
   477	```xml
   478	<w:del w:id="2" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   479	  <w:r><w:delText>deleted text</w:delText></w:r>
   480	</w:del>
   481	```
   482	
   483	**Inside `<w:del>`**: Use `<w:delText>` instead of `<w:t>`, and `<w:delInstrText>` instead of `<w:instrText>`.
   484	
   485	**Minimal edits** - only mark what changes:
   486	```xml
   487	<!-- Change "30 days" to "60 days" -->
   488	<w:r><w:t>The term is </w:t></w:r>
   489	<w:del w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="...">
   490	  <w:r><w:delText>30</w:delText></w:r>
   491	</w:del>
   492	<w:ins w:id="2" w:author="Claude" w:date="...">
   493	  <w:r><w:t>60</w:t></w:r>
   494	</w:ins>
   495	<w:r><w:t> days.</w:t></w:r>
   496	```
   497	
   498	**Deleting entire paragraphs/list items** - when removing ALL content from a paragraph, also mark the paragraph mark as deleted so it merges with the next paragraph. Add `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>`:
   499	```xml
   500	<w:p>
   501	  <w:pPr>
   502	    <w:numPr>...</w:numPr>  <!-- list numbering if present -->
   503	    <w:rPr>
   504	      <w:del w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z"/>
   505	    </w:rPr>
   506	  </w:pPr>
   507	  <w:del w:id="2" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   508	    <w:r><w:delText>Entire paragraph content being deleted...</w:delText></w:r>
   509	  </w:del>
   510	</w:p>
   511	```
   512	Without the `<w:del/>` in `<w:pPr><w:rPr>`, accepting changes leaves an empty paragraph/list item.
   513	
   514	**Rejecting another author's insertion** - nest deletion inside their insertion:
   515	```xml
   516	<w:ins w:author="Jane" w:id="5">
   517	  <w:del w:author="Claude" w:id="10">
   518	    <w:r><w:delText>their inserted text</w:delText></w:r>
   519	  </w:del>
   520	</w:ins>
   521	```
   522	
   523	**Restoring another author's deletion** - add insertion after (don't modify their deletion):
   524	```xml
   525	<w:del w:author="Jane" w:id="5">
   526	  <w:r><w:delText>deleted text</w:delText></w:r>
   527	</w:del>
   528	<w:ins w:author="Claude" w:id="10">
   529	  <w:r><w:t>deleted text</w:t></w:r>
   530	</w:ins>
   531	```
   532	
   533	### Comments
   534	
   535	After running `comment.py` (see Step 2), add markers to document.xml. For replies, use `--parent` flag and nest markers inside the parent's.
   536	
   537	**CRITICAL: `<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` are siblings of `<w:r>`, never inside `<w:r>`.**
   538	
   539	```xml
   540	<!-- Comment markers are direct children of w:p, never inside w:r -->
   541	<w:commentRangeStart w:id="0"/>
   542	<w:del w:id="1" w:author="Claude" w:date="2025-01-01T00:00:00Z">
   543	  <w:r><w:delText>deleted</w:delText></w:r>
   544	</w:del>
   545	<w:r><w:t> more text</w:t></w:r>
   546	<w:commentRangeEnd w:id="0"/>
   547	<w:r><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="CommentReference"/></w:rPr><w:commentReference w:id="0"/></w:r>
   548	
   549	<!-- Comment 0 with reply 1 nested inside -->
   550	<w:commentRangeStart w:id="0"/>
   551	  <w:commentRangeStart w:id="1"/>
   552	  <w:r><w:t>text</w:t></w:r>
   553	  <w:commentRangeEnd w:id="1"/>
   554	<w:commentRangeEnd w:id="0"/>
   555	<w:r><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="CommentReference"/></w:rPr><w:commentReference w:id="0"/></w:r>
   556	<w:r><w:rPr><w:rStyle w:val="CommentReference"/></w:rPr><w:commentReference w:id="1"/></w:r>
   557	```
   558	
   559	### Images
   560	
   561	1. Add image file to `word/media/`
   562	2. Add relationship to `word/_rels/document.xml.rels`:
   563	```xml
   564	<Relationship Id="rId5" Type=".../image" Target="media/image1.png"/>
   565	```
   566	3. Add content type to `[Content_Types].xml`:
   567	```xml
   568	<Default Extension="png" ContentType="image/png"/>
   569	```
   570	4. Reference in document.xml:
   571	```xml
   572	<w:drawing>
   573	  <wp:inline>
   574	    <wp:extent cx="914400" cy="914400"/>  <!-- EMUs: 914400 = 1 inch -->
   575	    <a:graphic>
   576	      <a:graphicData uri=".../picture">
   577	        <pic:pic>
   578	          <pic:blipFill><a:blip r:embed="rId5"/></pic:blipFill>
   579	        </pic:pic>
   580	      </a:graphicData>
   581	    </a:graphic>
   582	  </wp:inline>
   583	</w:drawing>
   584	```
   585	
   586	---
   587	
   588	## Dependencies
   589	
   590	- **pandoc**: Text extraction
   591	- **docx**: `npm install -g docx` (new documents)
   592	- **LibreOffice**: PDF conversion (auto-configured for sandboxed environments via `scripts/office/soffice.py`)
   593	- **Poppler**: `pdftoppm` for images
   594
Now I'll create a concise version of the resume with a more streamlined academic projects section and generate a Word document.

[12:10 PM, 5/10/2026] Saud CR 1C BUKC: Table of Contents: Resume Screening & Skill Extraction System
Introduction & Problem Definition (The real-world problem with traditional keyword-based screening)

System Objectives & Requirements (What the software must do, bridging Software Engineering & NLP)

Selected NLP Approach & Justification (Why we chose Semantic LLM over basic Regex/Spacy)

System Architecture & Data Flow (How raw PDF text is processed into structured data)

AI Interaction & Prompt Engineering (Showcasing the "Knockout Rule" and our conversations with AI)

Result Evidence & Performance Analysis (Demonstrating correctness, robustness, and JSON parsing success)

Representative Scenarios (Walkthrough of a perfect resume vs. a vocabulary-mismatched resume)

Live System Demonstration (The live walkthrough of the working portal)

Conclusion & Key Takeaways
[12:10 PM, 5/10/2026] Saud CR 1C BUKC: Slide 1: Introduction & Problem Definition
The Traditional Recruitment Bottleneck: Standard Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) rely on rigid, rule-based keyword matching (Regex) to screen resumes.

The "Vocabulary Mismatch" Problem: Highly qualified candidates are frequently rejected because they use different terminology than the job description (e.g., listing "PyTorch" and "TensorFlow" when the ATS is strictly searching for "Machine Learning").

Lack of Contextual Understanding: Traditional systems cannot distinguish between "used React for 5 years" and "want to learn React."

The Proposed Solution: A semantic "Resume Screening and Skill Extraction System" that uses advanced Natural Language Processing (NLP) to read, comprehend, and evaluate resumes based on conceptual meaning rather than exact string matches.

Slide 2: System Objectives & Requirements
Core Objective: To develop a fully functional NLP software system that accurately extracts text from PDF resumes, compares it semantically against a Job Description (JD), and outputs a reliable match score with constructive feedback.

Functional Requirements:

Data Ingestion: Must accurately extract raw text from unstructured PDF documents.

Semantic Matching: Must map candidate skills to job requirements contextually (e.g., recognizing that "Node.js" fulfills a "Backend Developer" requirement).

Structured Output: The NLP engine must return predictable, strictly formatted JSON data to ensure seamless integration with the frontend UI.

Non-Functional Requirements: Low latency (fast processing time), robust error handling (preventing AI hallucinations), and high availability.

Slide 3: Selected NLP Approach & Justification
Why not basic Regex or TF-IDF? Simple word-frequency models (TF-IDF) or Named Entity Recognition (NER) cannot understand relationships between tech stacks or context.

The Selected Approach: Large Language Models (LLMs) with advanced Prompt Engineering (specifically GPT-4o-mini).

Justification for this Approach:

Semantic Reasoning: LLMs inherently understand tech ecosystems (Skill Knowledge Graphs) and can deduce that a "MERN stack" developer satisfies requirements for MongoDB, Express, React, and Node.

Instruction Following (Zero-Shot Prompting): We can instruct the model to act as a "Strict ATS Evaluator" without needing to train a custom model from scratch.

Cost-to-Performance Ratio: Using a lightweight LLM ensures fast real-time processing and low API costs while maintaining high reasoning capabilities.

Slide 4: System Architecture & Data Flow
Step 1: Document Processing: The user uploads a PDF. The backend utilizes the pdf2json event-driven library to strip formatting and extract raw, unstructured text.

Step 2: Data Assembly: The server combines the raw Resume Text and the Employer's Job Description into a single, comprehensive payload.

Step 3: The NLP Layer: The payload is sent to the AI API along with a strict system prompt.

Step 4: JSON Extraction: The backend receives the AI's response, runs a custom Regex filter to isolate the JSON data block (ignoring any conversational text), and parses it.

Step 5: Frontend Rendering: The structured data (Match Score, Pros, Cons) is sent to the React.js frontend to be displayed dynamically.

Slide 5: AI Interaction & Prompt Engineering
Constraint Design: AI models can be unpredictable. We engineered the prompt to enforce strict boundaries.

The "Knockout Rule": We specifically programmed the NLP prompt to penalize missing core technical skills heavily. (e.g., Prompt instruction: "If foundational technical skills are missing, score must be below 40%, regardless of soft skills.")

Semantic Cluster Instruction: Prompt instruction: "Solve vocabulary mismatch. Do not rely on exact keyword matching. Recognize context and underlying skill clusters."

Output Formatting: Prompt instruction: "CRITICAL: Return EXACTLY a valid JSON object with the keys: parsedResumeSummary, score, pros, cons. Do not include markdown or conversational text."

Slide 6: Result Evidence & Performance Analysis
Parsing Robustness: The pdf2json pipeline successfully extracts text from 95%+ of standard resumes, effectively ignoring complex multi-column layouts that break older parsers.

Semantic Accuracy: In experimental testing, the system successfully matched synonyms (e.g., "UI/UX" mapped to "Frontend Design") and caught contextual negative matches (e.g., "Familiar with Python" vs "Expert in Python").

System Reliability: By combining strict LLM prompting with a backend JSON-extraction Regex, we reduced UI crash rates due to "AI Hallucinations" to near 0%.

Latency Analysis: The entire process—from PDF upload to displaying the final UI score—averages under 5 seconds, making it highly viable for real-world HR usage.

Slide 7: Representative Scenarios (The Edge Cases)
Scenario A: The Perfect Match: A resume explicitly lists all required JD skills. Result: High score (90%+), exact matches identified in "Pros".

Scenario B: The Vocabulary Mismatch (The System's Strength): JD asks for "Object-Oriented Programming" and "Relational Databases." Resume lists "Java" and "MySQL." Result: The NLP engine successfully bridges the semantic gap, scoring the candidate highly and explaining the connection in the feedback.

Scenario C: The "Fluff" Resume: Candidate lists strong leadership and teamwork but lacks the required "React.js" skill. Result: The "Knockout Rule" triggers. Low score (30%), with feedback explicitly stating the missing core technical requirement.

Slide 8: Live System Demonstration
(Speaker Notes: This is where you switch screens and show the actual HireAI portal.)

Demonstrate uploading a PDF resume.

Show the system analyzing it against a Job Description.

Highlight the final output: The Score, the extracted text, the Pros, and the Cons.

(Optional but impressive): Show the teacher the "Network Tab" or your terminal logs to prove the raw JSON data is actually being generated by the NLP engine.

Slide 9: Conclusion & Key Takeaways
Bridging Software Engineering and NLP: We successfully integrated an advanced NLP model into a full-stack MERN application, creating a tangible, real-world product.

Beyond Keywords: We proved that semantic reasoning is vastly superior to traditional Regex/String matching for evaluating human talent.

The Power of Explainability: By extracting structured "Pros" and "Cons", we transformed a traditional "Black Box" screening tool into a transparent, educational platform for job seekers.

display_content.text: Reading the file-reading skill to understand how to properly read the PPTX file

content.text:
1	---
     2	name: file-reading
     3	description: "Use this skill when a file has been uploaded but its content is NOT in your context — only its path at /mnt/user-data/uploads/ is listed in an uploaded_files block. This skill is a router: it tells you which tool to use for each file type (pdf, docx, xlsx, csv, json, images, archives, ebooks) so you read the right amount the right way instead of blindly running cat on a binary. Triggers: any mention of /mnt/user-data/uploads/, an uploaded_files section, a file_path tag, or a user asking about an uploaded file you have not yet read. Do NOT use this skill if the file content is already visible in your context inside a documents block — you already have it."
     4	compatibility: "claude.ai, Claude Desktop, Cowork — any surface where uploads land at /mnt/user-data/uploads/"
     5	license: Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms
     6	---
     7	
     8	# Reading Uploaded Files
     9	
    10	## Why this skill exists
    11	
    12	When a user uploads a file in claude.ai, Claude Desktop, or Cowork,
    13	the file is written to `/mnt/user-data/uploads/<filename>` and you are told the path
    14	in an `<uploaded_files>` block. **The content is not in your context.**
    15	You must go read it.
    16	
    17	The naive thing — `cat /mnt/user-data/uploads/whatever` — is wrong for
    18	most files:
    19	
    20	- On a PDF it prints binary garbage.
    21	- On a 100MB CSV it floods your context with rows you will never use.
    22	- On a DOCX it prints the raw ZIP bytes.
    23	- On an image it does nothing useful at all.
    24	
    25	This skill tells you the right first move for each type, and when to
    26	hand off to a deeper skill.
    27	
    28	## General protocol
    29	
    30	1. **Look at the extension.** That is your dispatch key.
    31	2. **Stat before you read.** Large files need sampling, not slurping.
    32	   ```bash
    33	   stat -c '%s bytes, %y' /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf
    34	   file /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf
    35	   ```
    36	3. **Read just enough to answer the user's question.** If they asked
    37	   "how many rows are in this CSV", don't load the whole thing into
    38	   pandas — `wc -l` gives a fast approximation (it counts newlines,
    39	   not CSV records, so it may over-count if quoted fields contain
    40	   embedded newlines).
    41	4. **If a dedicated skill exists, go read it.** The table below tells
    42	   you when. The dedicated skills cover editing, creating, and advanced
    43	   operations that this skill does not.
    44	
    45	## `extract-text`
    46	
    47	For docx, odt, epub, xlsx, pptx, rtf, and ipynb the first move is
    48	`extract-text <file>`. It emits markdown for docx/odt/epub (headings,
    49	bold, lists, links, tables), tab-separated rows under `## Sheet:`
    50	headers for xlsx, text under `## Slide N` headers for pptx, fenced
    51	code cells for ipynb, and plain text for rtf. Pass `--format <fmt>`
    52	when the extension is wrong or absent (e.g., `--format xlsx` on an
    53	`.xlsm`). If it errors on a file, `pandoc <file> -t plain` is a
    54	fallback; for xlsx/pptx, fall back to the dedicated skill's
    55	Python-based approach (openpyxl / python-pptx).
    56	
    57	## Dispatch table
    58	
    59	| Extension                         | First move                                           | Dedicated skill                           |
    60	| --------------------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------- |
    61	| `.pdf`                            | Content inventory (see PDF section)                  | `/mnt/skills/public/pdf-reading/SKILL.md` |
    62	| `.docx`                           | `extract-text`                                       | `/mnt/skills/public/docx/SKILL.md`        |
    63	| `.doc` (legacy)                   | Convert to `.docx` first                             | `/mnt/skills/public/docx/SKILL.md`        |
    64	| `.xlsx`                           | `extract-text`                                       | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |
    65	| `.xlsm`                           | `extract-text --format xlsx`                         | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |
    66	| `.xls` (legacy)                   | `pd.read_excel(engine="xlrd")` — openpyxl rejects it | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |
    67	| `.ods`                            | `pd.read_excel(engine="odf")` — openpyxl rejects it  | `/mnt/skills/public/xlsx/SKILL.md`        |
    68	| `.pptx`                           | `extract-text`                                       | `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`        |
    69	| `.ppt` (legacy)                   | Convert to `.pptx` first                             | `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`        |
    70	| `.csv`, `.tsv`                    | `pandas` with `nrows`                                | — (below)                                 |
    71	| `.json`, `.jsonl`                 | `jq` for structure                                   | — (below)                                 |
    72	| `.jpg`, `.png`, `.gif`, `.webp`   | Already in your context as vision input              | — (below)                                 |
    73	| `.zip`, `.tar`, `.tar.gz`         | List contents, do **not** auto-extract               | — (below)                                 |
    74	| `.gz` (single file)               | `zcat \| head` — no manifest to list                 | — (below)                                 |
    75	| `.epub`, `.odt`                   | `extract-text`                                       | — (below)                                 |
    76	| `.rtf`                            | `extract-text`                                       | — (below)                                 |
    77	| `.ipynb`                          | `extract-text`                                       | — (below)                                 |
    78	| `.txt`, `.md`, `.log`, code files | `wc -c` then `head` or full `cat`                    | — (below)                                 |
    79	| Unknown                           | `file` then decide                                   | —                                         |
    80	
    81	---
    82	
    83	## PDF
    84	
    85	**Never** `cat` a PDF — it prints binary garbage.
    86	
    87	Quick first move — get the page count and check if text is extractable:
    88	
    89	```bash
    90	pdfinfo /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf
    91	pdftotext -f 1 -l 1 /mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf - | head -20
    92	```
    93	
    94	Then peek at the text content:
    95	
    96	```python
    97	from pypdf import PdfReader
    98	r = PdfReader("/mnt/user-data/uploads/report.pdf")
    99	print(f"{len(r.pages)} pages")
   100	print(r.pages[0].extract_text()[:2000])
   101	```
   102	
   103	For anything beyond a quick peek — figures, tables, attachments,
   104	forms, scanned PDFs, visual inspection, or choosing a reading strategy
   105	— go read `/mnt/skills/public/pdf-reading/SKILL.md`. It covers
   106	content inventory, text extraction vs. page rasterization, embedded
   107	content extraction, and document-type-aware reading strategies.
   108	
   109	For PDF form filling, creation, merging, splitting, or watermarking,
   110	go read `/mnt/skills/public/pdf/SKILL.md`.
   111	
   112	---
   113	
   114	## DOCX / DOC
   115	
   116	The `docx` skill covers editing, creating, tracked changes, images.
   117	Read it if you need any of those. For a quick look:
   118	
   119	```bash
   120	extract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/memo.docx | head -200
   121	```
   122	
   123	Legacy `.doc` (not `.docx`) must be converted first — see the `docx`
   124	skill.
   125	
   126	---
   127	
   128	## XLSX / XLS / spreadsheets
   129	
   130	The `xlsx` skill covers formulas, formatting, charts, creating. Read
   131	it if you need any of those. For a quick look at an `.xlsx`:
   132	
   133	```bash
   134	extract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.xlsx | head -100
   135	```
   136	
   137	For `.xlsm`, add `--format xlsx` (same zip structure; only the
   138	extension differs). When you need a structured preview in Python:
   139	
   140	```python
   141	from openpyxl import load_workbook
   142	wb = load_workbook("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.xlsx", read_only=True)
   143	print("Sheets:", wb.sheetnames)
   144	ws = wb.active
   145	for row in ws.iter_rows(max_row=5, values_only=True):
   146	    print(row)
   147	```
   148	
   149	`read_only=True` matters — without it, openpyxl loads the entire
   150	workbook into memory, which breaks on large files. Do not trust
   151	`ws.max_row` in read-only mode: many non-Excel writers omit the
   152	dimension record, so it comes back `None` or wrong. If you need a row
   153	count, iterate or use pandas.
   154	
   155	**Legacy `.xls`** — openpyxl raises `InvalidFileException`. Use:
   156	
   157	```python
   158	import pandas as pd
   159	df = pd.read_excel("/mnt/user-data/uploads/old.xls", engine="xlrd", nrows=5)
   160	```
   161	
   162	**`.ods` (OpenDocument)** — openpyxl also rejects this. Use:
   163	
   164	```python
   165	import pandas as pd
   166	df = pd.read_excel("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.ods", engine="odf", nrows=5)
   167	```
   168	
   169	---
   170	
   171	## PPTX
   172	
   173	```bash
   174	extract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/deck.pptx | head -200
   175	```
   176	
   177	**Legacy `.ppt`** — convert to `.pptx` first via LibreOffice; see
   178	`/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md` for the sandbox-safe
   179	`scripts/office/soffice.py` wrapper (bare `soffice` hangs here because
   180	the seccomp filter blocks the `AF_UNIX` sockets LibreOffice uses for
   181	instance management).
   182	
   183	For anything beyond reading, go to `/mnt/skills/public/pptx/SKILL.md`.
   184	
   185	---
   186	
   187	## CSV / TSV
   188	
   189	**Do not** `cat` or `head` these blindly. A CSV with a 50KB quoted cell
   190	in row 1 will wreck your `head -5`. Use pandas with `nrows`:
   191	
   192	```python
   193	import pandas as pd
   194	df = pd.read_csv("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv", nrows=5)
   195	print(df)
   196	print()
   197	print(df.dtypes)
   198	```
   199	
   200	Approximate row count without loading (over-counts if the file has
   201	RFC-4180 quoted newlines — the same quoted-cell case this section
   202	warned about above):
   203	
   204	```bash
   205	wc -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv
   206	```
   207	
   208	Full analysis only after you know the shape:
   209	
   210	```python
   211	df = pd.read_csv("/mnt/user-data/uploads/data.csv")
   212	print(df.describe())
   213	```
   214	
   215	TSV: same, with `sep="\t"`.
   216	
   217	---
   218	
   219	## JSON / JSONL
   220	
   221	Structure first, content second:
   222	
   223	```bash
   224	jq 'type' /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json
   225	jq 'if type == "array" then length elif type == "object" then keys else . end' /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json
   226	```
   227	
   228	(`keys` errors on scalar JSON roots — a bare `"hello"` or `42` is valid
   229	JSON per RFC 7159 — so guard the branch.)
   230	
   231	Then drill into what the user actually asked about.
   232	
   233	JSONL (one object per line) — do **not** `jq` the whole file; work line
   234	by line:
   235	
   236	```bash
   237	head -3 /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.jsonl | jq .
   238	wc -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.jsonl
   239	```
   240	
   241	---
   242	
   243	## Images (JPG / PNG / GIF / WEBP)
   244	
   245	**You can already see uploaded images.** They are injected into your
   246	context as vision inputs alongside the `<uploaded_files>` pointer. You
   247	do not need to read them from disk to describe them.
   248	
   249	The disk copy is only needed if you are going to **process** the image
   250	programmatically:
   251	
   252	```python
   253	from PIL import Image
   254	img = Image.open("/mnt/user-data/uploads/photo.jpg")
   255	print(img.size, img.mode, img.format)
   256	```
   257	
   258	For OCR on an image (text extraction, not description):
   259	
   260	```python
   261	import pytesseract
   262	print(pytesseract.image_to_string(img))
   263	```
   264	
   265	Note: the client resizes images larger than 2000×2000 down to that
   266	bound and re-encodes as JPEG before upload, so the disk copy may not
   267	be the user's original bytes. For most processing this doesn't matter;
   268	if the user is asking about original-resolution pixel data, flag it.
   269	
   270	---
   271	
   272	## Archives (ZIP / TAR / TAR.GZ)
   273	
   274	**List first. Extract never — unless the user explicitly asks.**
   275	Archives can be huge, contain path traversal, or nest forever.
   276	
   277	```bash
   278	unzip -l /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.zip
   279	tar -tf /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.tar
   280	```
   281	
   282	GNU tar auto-detects compression — `tar -tf` works on `.tar`,
   283	`.tar.gz`, `.tar.bz2`, `.tar.xz` alike. Don't hard-code `-z`.
   284	
   285	If the user wants one file from inside, extract just that one:
   286	
   287	```bash
   288	unzip -p /mnt/user-data/uploads/bundle.zip path/inside/file.txt
   289	```
   290	
   291	**Standalone `.gz`** (not a tar) compresses a single file — there is
   292	no manifest to list. Just peek at the decompressed content:
   293	
   294	```bash
   295	zcat /mnt/user-data/uploads/data.json.gz | head -50
   296	```
   297	
   298	---
   299	
   300	## EPUB / ODT
   301	
   302	```bash
   303	extract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/book.epub | head -200
   304	```
   305	
   306	For long ebooks, pipe through `head` — you rarely need the whole thing
   307	to answer a question.
   308	
   309	---
   310	
   311	## RTF / IPYNB
   312	
   313	```bash
   314	extract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/notes.rtf | head -200
   315	extract-text /mnt/user-data/uploads/notebook.ipynb | head -200
   316	```
   317	
   318	---
   319	
   320	## Plain text / code / logs
   321	
   322	Check the size first:
   323	
   324	```bash
   325	wc -c /mnt/user-data/uploads/app.log
   326	```
   327	
   328	- **Under ~20KB**: `cat` is fine.
   329	- **Over ~20KB**: `head -100` and `tail -100` to orient. If the user
   330	  asked about something specific, `grep` for it. Load the whole thing
   331	  only if you genuinely need all of it.
   332	
   333	For log files, the user almost always cares about the end:
   334	
   335	```bash
   336	tail -200 /mnt/user-data/uploads/app.log
   337	```
   338	
   339	---
   340	
   341	## Unknown extension
   342	
   343	```bash
   344	file /mnt/user-data/uploads/mystery.bin
   345	xxd /mnt/user-data/uploads/mystery.bin | head -5
   346	```
   347	
   348	`file` identifies most things. `xxd` head shows magic bytes. If `file`
   349	says "data" and the hex doesn't match anything you recognize, ask the
   350	user what it is instead of guessing.
   351

## Slide 2

Table of Contents

Project Recap & The Core Mission
Final System Architecture & Tech Stack
The Core Modules:
Module 1: The Semantic Intelligence Engine (AI Resume Auditor)
Module 2: Explainable AI & Smart Feedback
Module 3: The Real-Time Ecosystem & AI Assistant
Live Application Walkthrough (Demo) 
Key Technical Challenges & Solutions
Deployment, Testing & Security
Conclusion & Project Impact

## Slide 3

Project Recap & The Core Mission

The Industry Problem:
Traditional Applicant Tracking Systems (ATS) rely on rigid keyword matching (e.g., rejecting a candidate who wrote "TensorFlow" when the JD asked for "Machine Learning").
The "Black Box" Rejection: Candidates receive automated rejections with zero actionable feedback, hindering their professional growth.
The Mission of HireAI:
To build an intelligent, context-aware recruitment platform that bridges the gap between job descriptions and resumes using Semantic Analysis rather than simple string matching.
The Solution Scope:
A dual-sided portal that empowers employers with automated, AI-generated job postings and real-time applicant tracking, while providing candidates with Explainable AI (XAI) feedback to improve their employability.

## Slide 4

Final System Architecture & Tech Stack

Frontend Ecosystem (The User Interface):
React.js + Vite: Chosen for fast 	Hot Module Replacement (HMR) and highly optimized production builds.
Redux Toolkit: Used for centralized, predictable state management (handling active user sessions, role-based UI toggles, and live chat states).
Tailwind CSS: For a highly responsive, modern, and component-driven styling architecture.
Backend Ecosystem (The Engine):
Node.js & Express.js: A lightweight, non-blocking asynchronous server handling RESTful API requests.
MongoDB & Mongoose: A flexible NoSQL database schema designed to handle complex nested data (e.g., User Profiles containing arrays of applied jobs and embedded AI analysis objects).
The Intelligence & External Layers:
OpenAI LLM: The core AI engine accessed via API for semantic reasoning and text generation.
JSearch API (RapidAPI): Powers the "Hybrid Search Engine," pulling live external market data and normalizing it to match our internal database schema.

## Slide 5

The Core Modules:

Module 1: The Semantic Intelligence Engine (AI Resume Auditor)
Event-Driven PDF Parsing (pdf2json):
Replaced basic text extractors with a robust, event-driven parser that accurately strips text from user-uploaded PDFs (Resumes) without losing critical formatting.
Files are securely uploaded to Cloudinary for permanent cloud storage while the raw buffer is fed to the AI.
Solving the "Vocabulary Mismatch":
The AI evaluates candidates based on conceptual clusters (e.g., mapping "React.js" as a valid child node of "Frontend Development").
The "Knockout Rule" Logic:
The engine is programmed with strict parameters. If a candidate lacks the core technical stack (e.g., C# for a .NET role), the algorithm automatically caps their score below 40%. Soft skills cannot artificially inflate a score if foundational tech is missing.
Strict JSON Enforcement:
Through advanced prompt engineering and backend Regex parsing, the LLM is forced to return a strictly typed JSON object, preventing application crashes caused by "AI hallucinations" or conversational text.

## Slide 6

The Core Modules:

Module 2: Explainable AI & Smart Feedback
Demystifying the Rejection (Candidate Side):
The platform translates raw AI match scores into human-readable, actionable feedback.
Generates specific "Pros" (validating what the candidate did right) and "Cons" (explicitly listing the core technical skills or experience gaps that cost them the job).
Automated Job Generation (Employer Side):
Employers input raw, fragmented data (e.g., Title: Dev, Salary: 50k, Skills: React, Node etc.).
The AI dynamically formats this into a highly professional, beautifully structured Job Description (JD) with sections for Responsibilities, Technical Requirements, and Benefits.
Dynamic Status Workflows:
When an employer changes a candidate's status to "Shortlisted" or "Rejected," the system allows them to append custom reasoning, which triggers an automated notification to the job seeker's dashboard.

## Slide 7

The Core Modules:

Module 3: The Real-Time Ecosystem & AI Assistant
Dynamic Analytics & Expiration Enforcement:
Built a robust backend script that normalizes timezones and strips hours/minutes to strictly compare current dates against job deadlines.
Jobs past their deadline are automatically removed from "Active Listings," grayed out in the UI, branded with a "Deadline Crossed" tag, and block new applications.
Real-Time Live Messaging (Socket.io):
Implemented full-duplex, bidirectional communication channels between employers and shortlisted candidates.
Features include live "Online/Offline" presence indicators, unread message badges, and instant UI updates without page reloads.
The Hybrid Job Discovery Engine:
Users can seamlessly toggle between jobs posted directly on HireAI (Internal Database) and live market jobs pulled from LinkedIn/Glassdoor (External API), heavily increasing the platform's utility from Day 1.
AI Assistant
Job seekers can interact with the AI freely, discussing a wide range of general topics with strict constraints, acting as a versatile resource for everyday queries and support.
Eg: if selected “Interview Prepper Mode”, it will act strictly as interview mode and wont answer casually but professionally and train you for the interview.

## Slide 8

Live Application Walkthrough (Demo)

## Slide 9

Live Application Walkthrough (Demo)

Resume Auditor

## Slide 10

Live Application Walkthrough (Demo)

## Slide 11

Live Application Walkthrough (Demo)

Employer Evaluating Job Seeker

## Slide 12

Key Technical Challenges & Solutions

Challenge 1: Taming the AI for Fair & Consistent Grading
Problem: AI models can be unpredictable. Early on, the AI would sometimes give conversational answers instead of raw data, or it would inflate candidate scores just because a resume looked nice, ignoring the actual technical skills.
Solution: We implemented strict "Prompt Engineering." We introduced the "Knockout Rule" to force the AI to penalize missing core skills. We also built a custom backend parser to force the AI to return strictly formatted JSON data, ensuring our app's UI never crashed from unexpected text.
Challenge 2: The "Empty Platform" Problem (Hybrid Data Integration)
Problem: A brand-new job portal is useless if it has no jobs. We needed a way to combine jobs posted internally on our platform with live, real-world jobs from the internet (APIs)—but external data is messy and comes in vastly different formats.
Solution: We built a "Translator" feature in our backend. It intercepts the messy data from the live internet (JSearch API), instantly cleans it, and reshapes it to perfectly match our internal MongoDB database structure so the frontend cards always look perfect.
Challenge 3: Building a Seamless Real-Time Ecosystem
Problem: Implementing live, instant messaging and real-time notifications is heavy on the system. The app struggled to keep track of who was online, unread messages, and updating the UI without forcing the user to refresh the page.
Solution: We combined Socket.io (for instant two-way communication) with Redux Toolkit (to act as the app's central memory). This ensured that chat histories, live statuses, and employer notifications updated instantly and smoothly across every page of the application.

## Slide 13

Deployment, Testing & Security

Cloud Architecture:
Frontend: Deployed on Vercel and and NameCheap.
Backend: Hosted on a VPS/Cloud provider using PM2 as the production process manager to ensure zero-downtime restarts and log monitoring on Digital Ocean.
Database: MongoDB Atlas for managed, secure cloud data storage.
Enterprise-Grade Security:
Authentication: Stateless, highly secure session management using JWT (JSON Web Tokens) stored in HTTP-only cookies.
Data Protection: User passwords heavily encrypted via Bcrypt.js before ever touching the database.
Route Protection: Strict Express middleware validates user roles (Employer vs. Job Seeker) before granting access to sensitive API endpoints.

## Slide 14

Conclusion

Delivering on the Promise: HireAI successfully evolved from a conceptual prototype into a fully deployed, high-performance application.
Impact on Employers: Drastically reduces the time-to-hire by automating initial resume screening and highlighting top-tier candidates with AI accuracy.
Impact on Candidates: Eliminates the "Black Box" by providing real, explainable feedback, turning every rejection into a learning opportunity.
The Future Vision:
Integrating automated Video Interview analysis.
Integrate more features making it more scalable and include pricing for advance features

## Slide 15

Challenge 2: The "Empty Platform" Problem (Hybrid Data Integration)
Problem: A brand-new job portal is useless if it has no jobs. We needed a way to combine jobs posted internally on our platform with live, real-world jobs from the internet (APIs)—but external data is messy and comes in vastly different formats.
Solution: We built a "Translator" feature in our backend. It intercepts the messy data from the live internet (JSearch API), instantly cleans it, and reshapes it to perfectly match our internal MongoDB database structure so the frontend cards always look perfect.
Challenge 3: Building a Seamless Real-Time Ecosystem
Problem: Implementing live, instant messaging and real-time notifications is heavy on the system. The app struggled to keep track of who was online, unread messages, and updating the UI without forcing the user to refresh the page.
Solution: We combined Socket.io (for instant two-way communication) with Redux Toolkit (to act as the app's central memory). This ensured that chat histories, live statuses, and employer notifications updated instantly and smoothly across every page of the application.

## Slide 13

Deployment, Testing & Security

Cloud Architecture:
Frontend: Deployed on Vercel and and NameCheap.
Backend: Hosted on a VPS/Cloud provider using PM2 as the production process manager to ensure zero-downtime restarts and log monitoring on Digital Ocean.
Database: MongoDB Atlas for managed, secure cloud data storage.
Enterprise-Grade Security:
Authentication: Stateless, highly secure session management using JWT (JSON Web Tokens) stored in HTTP-only cookies.
Data Protection: User passwords heavily encrypted via Bcrypt.js before ever touching the database.
Route Protection: Strict Express middleware validates user roles (Employer vs. Job Seeker) before granting access to sensitive API endpoints.

## Slide 14

Conclusion

Delivering on the Promise: HireAI successfully evolved from a conceptual prototype into a fully deployed, high-performance application.
Impact on Employers: Drastically reduces the time-to-hire by automating initial resume screening and highlighting top-tier candidates with AI accuracy.
Impact on Candidates: Eliminates the "Black Box" by providing real, explainable feedback, turning every rejection into a learning opportunity.
The Future Vision:
Integrating automated Video Interview analysis.
Integrate more features making it more scalable and include pricing for advance features

## Slide 15

Thankyou..!!","stderr":""}
