{"returncode":0,"stdout":"**HireAI****: An Intelligent Job Portal with Semantic Matching,**

**Explainable AI, and Fairness-Aware Ranking**

**Syed Abdul Rafay**

*Dept. of Software Engineering, Bahria University Karachi, Pakistan*

sarafay2003@gmail.com

**M. Saud Al Faisal**

*Dept. of Software Engineering, Bahria University Karachi, Pakistan*

saudalfaisal65@gmail.com

**Faisal Wahab**

*Dept. of Software Engineering, Bahria University Karachi, Pakistan*

faisalwahab256@gmail.com

*Supervisor: Engr. Zoobia Zeeshan | Dept. of Software Engineering, Bahria University Karachi*

**Abstract**

**Abstract—**Traditional job portals suffer from three critical systemic failures: vocabulary mismatch in resume-to-job matching, algorithmic bias in candidate ranking, and opaque rejection processes that leave applicants without actionable feedback. HireAI addresses these shortcomings through a full-stack MERN (MongoDB, Express.js, React, Node.js) application tightly integrated with a Python FastAPI AI microservice. The platform implements four core AI modules: (1) a Semantic Intelligence Engine leveraging the ESCO taxonomy to achieve 87.3% synonym-matching accuracy; (2) an Explainable AI Engine that generates counterfactual explanations for every match score; (3) a Fairness-Aware Ranking algorithm achieving a demographic parity ratio of 0.83; and (4) an NLP-based Resume Analyzer with ATS scoring and structured improvement feedback. Additionally, HireAI incorporates a Hybrid Job Discovery Engine aggregating real-time listings from LinkedIn, Indeed, and Google alongside internal postings, and an AI Career Chatbot supporting interview preparation and career guidance. User Acceptance Testing with 13 participants demonstrates 80% comprehension of XAI explanations and measurable improvements in recruitment efficiency. The system achieves sub-2-second AI inference, zero-cost deployment via free-tier cloud infrastructure, and direct alignment with UN Sustainable Development Goals 4, 8, 9, and 10.

**Keywords—***Explainable AI; Fairness-Aware Machine Learning; Semantic Skill Matching; NLP Resume Parsing; ATS Scoring; Hybrid Job Discovery; ESCO Taxonomy; IBM AIF360; Counterfactual Explanations; Recruitment Automation*

# I. Introduction

The global labor market is undergoing rapid digitalization, with online recruitment platforms now mediating the majority of formal employment matches in both developed and emerging economies. Despite this transformation, leading platforms such as LinkedIn, Indeed, and Rozee.pk remain fundamentally constrained by keyword-based matching architectures that were designed for the pre-machine-learning era. Three systemic failures persist across virtually all commercial platforms.

First, vocabulary mismatch causes significant false negatives: a candidate listing TensorFlow may not match a position requiring Machine Learning, despite the conceptual equivalence of these skills. Industry estimates suggest that keyword-based systems may miss up to 40% of genuinely qualified candidates due to terminology inconsistency [1]. Second, automated ranking algorithms trained on historical hiring data can perpetuate and amplify pre-existing demographic biases without any detection or mitigation mechanism. Third, rejected applicants universally receive no feedback on improvement pathways, creating a structurally opaque process that undermines both candidate development and platform trust.

These failures impose measurable costs. Employers conduct longer hiring cycles and incur higher recruitment costs when qualified candidates are filtered out by inadequate matching. Candidates invest substantial effort in applications without receiving actionable guidance. At a societal level, algorithmic bias in hiring systems contributes to the reproduction of structural inequalities in labor market access.

HireAI is developed as a Final Year Project at Bahria University Karachi to address these systemic deficiencies. The platform reframes recruitment as a semantic understanding and fairness governance problem, not merely a keyword retrieval task. It introduces transparency through counterfactual XAI explanations, enforces demographic parity through post-processing fairness-aware re-ranking, and aggregates job discovery across fragmented platforms.

***[📷 IMAGE******: ******HireAI****** System Overview / Hero Screenshot — show the main landing page or dashboard of the platform]***

*Fig. 1. **HireAI** platform overview: unified interface for job seekers, employers, and administrators.*

## A. Problem Statement

Current job portal systems fail across three analytically distinct dimensions:

- Vocabulary Mismatch: Keyword-based systems produce false negatives when candidates express skills using synonymous terminology. This is not merely a surface-level inconvenience but a structurally significant error source that disproportionately affects candidates who learned skills in non-English educational environments or who use domain-specific jargon.

- Algorithmic Bias: Automated ranking systems trained on historical hiring outcomes risk encoding and amplifying historical demographic disparities. Without fairness-aware mechanisms, selection rates for protected demographic groups may systematically fall below those of majority groups.

- Black-Box Rejection: Applicants receive no actionable feedback explaining rejection rationale. This absence of transparency violates both emerging legal requirements (GDPR Article 22) and fundamental principles of user-centered design, creating a cycle of uninformed reapplication without improvement.

## B. Research Objectives

This work pursues six primary objectives:

- Develop a Semantic Intelligence Engine achieving ≥85% accuracy in matching synonymous skill expressions through ESCO taxonomy integration and transformer-based embeddings.

- Implement Fairness-Aware Ranking with a demographic parity ratio exceeding 0.80 as measured by the Four-Fifths Rule.

- Build an Explainable AI Engine generating counterfactual match score explanations comprehensible to non-technical users.

- Deploy an NLP Resume Analyzer with sub-10-second ATS scoring, structured improvement feedback, and multi-format document support.

- Integrate a Hybrid Job Discovery Engine aggregating external job listings alongside internal postings under a unified interface.

- Deliver a production-ready, zero-cost-deployment system aligned with UN SDGs 4, 8, 9, and 10.

## C. Contributions

HireAI makes the following novel contributions to the recruitment technology domain:

- First production-grade integration of ESCO-based semantic skill matching with counterfactual XAI in a job portal system, validated through User Acceptance Testing.

- Implementation of IBM AIF360 post-processing fairness algorithm within a live recruitment ranking pipeline, achieving a demographic parity ratio of 0.83.

- Hybrid dual-mode job discovery architecture unifying internal and external listings under a single normalized interface without requiring direct API partnerships with platform operators.

- Comprehensive empirical evaluation demonstrating 87.3% semantic matching accuracy, 0.83 demographic parity ratio, 92.1% gender-coded language recall, and 80% XAI comprehension in structured UAT.

- Zero-cost deployment architecture demonstrating research-grade AI system deployment viability using exclusively free-tier cloud infrastructure.

# II. Related Work

The literature informing HireAI spans four intersecting domains: AI-assisted recruitment automation, explainable AI in high-stakes decision systems, fairness-aware machine learning in HR contexts, and NLP-based resume analysis. This section critically synthesizes key findings and identifies the gaps that HireAI addresses.

## A. AI-Assisted Recruitment Automation

The application of machine learning to recruitment processes has accelerated considerably since 2018. Li and Zhao (2021) demonstrated through an empirical study of automated resume screening systems that ML-based approaches can reduce initial screening time by up to 50% while simultaneously improving the precision of candidate quality metrics, as measured by interview-to-hire conversion rates [1]. Their analysis across 12 enterprise deployments found that ensemble methods combining content-based features (resume text) with behavioral signals (platform engagement) consistently outperformed single-modality systems.

Hybrid recommendation architectures have emerged as a dominant paradigm for job-candidate matching. Wilson (2023) showed that systems combining content-based filtering with collaborative signals achieve statistically significant improvements in hit rate and user satisfaction over single-method approaches [2]. However, Wilson's analysis also noted that collaborative filtering introduces a cold-start problem for new users and risks amplifying historical biases embedded in prior hiring decisions.

The application of large language models to structured resume information extraction has attracted significant recent attention. Ahmed (2022) conducted a systematic review finding that LLM-based approaches demonstrate competitive performance in skill identification, experience quantification, and education parsing, particularly on unstructured or non-standard resume formats [3]. However, LLM-based approaches involve non-trivial inference costs and latency challenges for production deployment, which HireAI addresses through selective use of LLMs for explanation generation rather than core matching computation.

A critical limitation shared across existing commercial platforms is the treatment of AI as a productivity tool without structured governance, explainability requirements, or fairness safeguards. Research-grade systems demonstrating state-of-the-art performance in controlled experiments typically lack the engineering completeness required for production deployment. HireAI bridges this gap by integrating governance, explainability, and fairness as first-class architectural requirements rather than post-hoc additions.

## B. Explainable AI in High-Stakes Decision Systems

The legal and ethical imperative for explainability in automated decision systems has been substantially strengthened by regulatory developments. The European Union's General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), specifically Article 22, grants individuals a legally enforceable right to explanation for automated decisions that significantly affect them [4]. Employment decisions fall squarely within this scope. The EU AI Act, adopted in 2024, further classifies AI-based recruitment tools as high-risk AI systems subject to mandatory transparency, logging, and human oversight requirements.

Within the spectrum of XAI techniques—including LIME, SHAP, attention visualization, and counterfactual explanations—counterfactual approaches have demonstrated superior practical utility in recruitment contexts. Wachter et al. (2018) established the theoretical foundation for counterfactual explanations without opening the black box, demonstrating that actionable recourse statements ('If you had certification X, your score would increase by Y points') provide more useful guidance to affected individuals than statistical attribution methods [5]. This insight directly motivates HireAI's XAI Engine design.

Despite these findings and legal requirements, counterfactual XAI remains systematically underimplemented in commercial recruitment platforms. Interviews with HR practitioners confirm that most platforms provide no explanation whatsoever for automated decisions. HireAI addresses this gap by treating explanation generation as a first-class system feature with measurable comprehension targets rather than an optional reporting layer.

***[📷 IMAGE******: XAI Explanation Screenshot — show the counterfactual explanation panel / match score breakdown UI for a candidate]***

*Fig. 2. **HireAI** Explainable AI Engine: counterfactual skill gap visualization and match score breakdown.*

## C. Fairness-Aware Machine Learning in Recruitment

Algorithmic bias in recruitment AI received widespread public and regulatory attention following the 2018 Reuters investigation into Amazon's internal AI recruiting tool, which exhibited systematic gender bias by penalizing resumes containing the word 'women's' and downgrading graduates of all-women's colleges [6]. This case established that ML systems trained on historical hiring data can encode and amplify pre-existing demographic disparities even when protected attributes are not directly used as features, through proxy variables and correlated signals.

The field has subsequently developed a taxonomy of quantitative fairness metrics. Hardt, Price, and Srebro (2016) formalized Demographic Parity, Equal Opportunity, and Equalized Odds as competing fairness criteria with distinct tradeoffs [7]. The U.S. Equal Employment Opportunity Commission's Four-Fifths Rule provides a legally grounded operational threshold: selection rates for protected groups must reach at least 80% of the rate for the highest-selected group. HireAI adopts this threshold as its primary fairness target.

Open-source toolkits have made bias detection and mitigation more accessible to practitioners. IBM's AI Fairness 360 provides implementations of 70+ fairness metrics and 10+ mitigation algorithms spanning pre-processing, in-processing, and post-processing approaches [8]. Microsoft's FairLearn similarly provides grid-search-based mitigation constrained on parity criteria. HireAI selects post-processing mitigation because it allows the semantic matching engine to operate without fairness constraints during inference, applying fairness correction only at the ranking stage, which preserves matching accuracy while achieving demographic parity objectives.

A significant gap in existing literature is the absence of production-validated implementations of fairness-aware ranking in operational recruitment platforms. Most published work presents evaluation results on benchmark datasets without deployment. HireAI addresses this by implementing and evaluating fairness-aware ranking in a live system tested with real employer participants.

## D. NLP-Based Resume Analysis and ATS Scoring

Named Entity Recognition for resume parsing has benefited substantially from transformer-based architectures. Garg, Kumar, and Sharma (2020) demonstrated that NER models fine-tuned on professional resume corpora achieve accuracy exceeding 90% on structured documents with consistent formatting, though performance degrades to approximately 75% on unstructured documents common among non-professional job seekers [9]. This performance gap has implications for equity: candidates without access to professional resume writing assistance may receive systematically lower ATS scores due to format variance rather than genuine skill deficiency.

Embedding-based similarity computation has substantially advanced beyond TF-IDF for vocabulary mismatch cases. Reimers and Gurevych (2019) introduced Sentence-BERT, demonstrating that siamese fine-tuned BERT networks produce semantically meaningful sentence embeddings for which cosine similarity captures genuine semantic relatedness [10]. This approach significantly outperforms TF-IDF on vocabulary mismatch cases — the core problem HireAI targets. HireAI's Semantic Engine uses the all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model, which provides an effective balance between semantic quality and inference speed suitable for sub-2-second response targets.

Commercial Applicant Tracking Systems represent a multi-billion dollar market segment but share a fundamental limitation: ATS scores are computed and reported without surfacing scoring rationale or actionable improvement pathways to candidates. This opacity creates an information asymmetry that disproportionately disadvantages candidates without access to professional coaching. HireAI's Resume Analyzer directly addresses this by providing explicit, component-level feedback for each scoring dimension.

***[📷 IMAGE******: Resume Analyzer / ATS Score Breakdown Screenshot — show the score components pie chart or bar chart and improvement suggestions panel]***

*Fig. 3. **HireAI** Resume Analyzer: ATS score breakdown with weighted component analysis and targeted improvement recommendations.*

# III. System Architecture

## A. High-Level Overview

HireAI follows a microservices-inspired architecture partitioned into three functionally distinct layers: a React.js single-page application frontend, a Node.js/Express.js API gateway backend, and a Python/FastAPI AI microservice. This separation enables independent scaling of AI workloads from web serving, leverages the Python ML ecosystem without sacrificing JavaScript backend consistency, and isolates AI inference latency from user-facing request handling.

The system supports four user roles: Guest (unauthenticated job browsing and platform discovery), Job Seeker (full application and profile features), Employer (job posting, candidate management, and bias audit tools), and Administrator (user management and system monitoring). Role-Based Access Control is enforced at the API gateway layer through JWT authentication middleware with role verification on every protected route.

| **Layer** | **Technology** | **Responsibility** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Frontend | React 18, Vite, Redux Toolkit | UI, state management, client-side routing |
| API Gateway | Node.js, Express.js | Authentication, business logic, request routing |
| AI Microservice | Python 3.11, FastAPI | Resume parsing, XAI, semantic matching, ranking |
| Database | MongoDB Atlas | Profiles, job listings, applications, sessions |
| File Storage | Cloudinary CDN | Resume storage, image hosting, format conversion |
| External APIs | JSearch (RapidAPI) | LinkedIn / Indeed / Glassdoor job aggregation |

*Table I. **HireAI** Technology Stack by System Layer*

***[📷 IMAGE******: System Architecture Diagram — a layered architecture diagram showing Frontend → API Gateway → AI Microservice → MongoDB, with arrows indicating data flow and the ******JSearch****** API integration]***

*Fig. 4. **HireAI** system architecture: three-layer microservices design with AI workload isolation.*

## B. Frontend Architecture

The React 18 frontend uses Vite for build tooling, Redux Toolkit for predictable state management, and the shadcn/ui component library built on Radix UI primitives for accessibility-compliant interaction patterns. React Router v6 handles client-side routing with protected route components enforcing role-based page access.

Application state is organized into domain-specific Redux slices: authentication and session state, job listing and search state, application lifecycle tracking, company profile data, and transient UI state. Redux Persist ensures session authentication survives page refreshes without requiring re-authentication. Tailwind CSS provides utility-first styling enabling consistent visual design across the 30+ component views.

## C. Backend Architecture

The Node.js backend follows a layered MVC pattern with clear separation between route definitions, controller logic, middleware, and data access layers. Express serves as the API gateway with JWT verification middleware applied to all authenticated routes, Multer-based multipart file upload processing for resume ingestion, and CORS configuration enabling frontend-backend communication during development and across deployment domains.

MongoDB Atlas with the Mongoose ODM provides schema definition and validation for a flexible document model well-suited to recruitment data's heterogeneous nested structures, including embedded resume content, variable-length skill arrays, and complex application lifecycle histories.

## D. AI Microservice Architecture

The Python FastAPI microservice exposes five primary endpoints, each serving a distinct AI workload:

- /parse-resume: Accepts PDF or DOCX uploads, returns structured JSON containing extracted skills, work experience, education, ATS score (0-100), and prioritized improvement suggestions.

- /match-score: Accepts resume text and job description, returns semantic similarity score with counterfactual explanation identifying the minimal skill additions for score improvement.

- /rerank-candidates: Accepts an initial match-ranked candidate list and optional demographic signals, returns a fairness-reranked list with demographic parity annotations.

- /chatbot: Accepts conversation history with injected user context, returns LLM-generated career guidance calibrated to the user's profile and application status.

- /analyze-job-post: Accepts a job description, returns bias detection results with flagged gender-coded phrases and neutral alternative suggestions.

# IV. Methodology

## A. Semantic Intelligence Engine

The Semantic Intelligence Engine addresses vocabulary mismatch through an ESCO (European Skills, Competences, Qualifications and Occupations) taxonomy-based skill knowledge graph. ESCO provides a structured multilingual hierarchy of 13,800+ occupation-related skills with defined synonym relationships, cross-domain mappings, and hierarchical concept organization — substantially richer than simple keyword synonym dictionaries.

Resume and job description texts are encoded using the Sentence Transformers library (all-MiniLM-L6-v2 model from HuggingFace Hub), producing 384-dimensional dense embedding vectors that capture semantic meaning beyond lexical surface similarity. The final semantic match score combines cosine similarity between embedding pairs with ESCO graph adjacency weights that boost scores for taxonomically related skills.

The knowledge graph is loaded from pre-processed JSON at microservice startup and parsed into a NetworkX directed graph. Skill matching uses a two-pass approach: first, exact and fuzzy string matching against ESCO node labels using the rapidfuzz library; second, embedding-based nearest-neighbor search using FAISS for cases where string matching confidence falls below threshold. This hybrid approach achieves both precision on exact skill mentions and recall on paraphrased or abbreviated skill references.

***[📷 IMAGE******: Semantic Matching Flow Diagram — a flowchart showing: Resume Text → Skill Extraction → ESCO Taxonomy Lookup + Embedding Encoding → FAISS Search → Cosine Similarity → Match Score]***

*Fig. 5. Semantic Intelligence Engine pipeline: ESCO taxonomy integration with transformer-based embedding matching.*

## B. Explainable AI Engine

The XAI Engine implements counterfactual explanation generation for all match scores. Given a candidate's current score and the gap to the job's minimum requirement threshold, the engine identifies the minimal set of skill additions that would produce a meaningful (≥10 percentage point) score increase — directly answering the question candidates care most about: 'What specifically would make me a stronger candidate?'

The algorithm computes feature-level contribution of each skill dimension using a simplified SHAP decomposition over the skill embedding space. Missing or weak-scoring skills are ranked by their marginal contribution to a hypothetically improved score. The top 3-5 actionable improvements are formatted into natural language explanation using an LLM, with the explanation grounded in specific, named skill acquisitions rather than abstract advice.

Engine output is a structured JSON object containing: the numerical match score, the primary reasons for the current score level, and specific skill acquisition recommendations with projected score impact for each. This is surfaced in the UI as both a visual Skill Contribution Chart and a natural language summary panel.

## C. Fairness-Aware Ranking

The Fairness-Aware Ranking (FAR) module implements post-processing bias mitigation on candidate shortlists generated by the Semantic Intelligence Engine. Post-processing is selected over pre-processing (data rebalancing) or in-processing (constrained optimization) approaches because it allows the semantic matching engine to optimize for relevance without fairness constraints during inference, applying correction only at the final ranking stage visible to employers.

After initial ranking by semantic match score, FAR applies IBM AIF360's Calibrated Equalized Odds post-processor in combination with FairLearn's grid search for equalized selection rates. The module targets Demographic Parity as measured by the Four-Fifths Rule: the selection rate for any protected group must be at least 80% of the highest-selected group's rate. Protected attribute proxies are inferred from anonymized candidate feature representations, avoiding direct collection of sensitive demographic data.

A complementary Employer-facing Bias Audit Dashboard surfaces longitudinal statistics on gender distribution across applicant pools, shortlisted candidates, and hired candidates. A pre-trained text classifier scans job descriptions for gender-coded language, flagging specific phrases with neutral alternatives drawn from the LIWC gender-coded word list.

## D. Resume Analyzer and ATS Scoring

The Resume Analyzer accepts PDF and DOCX files. PyMuPDF handles text extraction from PDF documents, python-docx processes DOCX files, and Tesseract OCR provides fallback processing for scanned or image-based PDFs — ensuring consistent analysis regardless of document digitization quality. Extracted text flows through a SpaCy NLP pipeline: sentence tokenization, Named Entity Recognition for personal information extraction, and a custom skill extraction component fine-tuned on software engineering and business administration resume datasets.

ATS score is a weighted composite across five dimensions: skills match against the ESCO-normalized taxonomy (40%), experience relevance and recency weighting (25%), education level and field alignment (20%), resume structural compliance with ATS-parseable formatting conventions (10%), and keyword density optimization (5%). Each component is scored independently and accompanied by specific, actionable improvement recommendations — directly addressing the opacity problem of commercial ATS systems.

## E. Hybrid Job Discovery Engine

The Hybrid Job Discovery Engine operates in two user-selectable modes switchable in real-time. Internal Mode queries MongoDB for employer-posted jobs fully integrated with ATS scoring and fairness-aware ranking. External Mode queries the JSearch API aggregating normalized listings from LinkedIn, Indeed, and Glassdoor.

In External Mode, the backend acts as a transparent proxy: it forwards user search parameters to JSearch, receives normalized JSON response objects, applies a field transformation layer (e.g., job_title → title, employer_name → company), and returns identical job card data structures to the frontend. Applying to external listings redirects users to the original platform posting URL, avoiding legal complexity while providing unified job discovery. This architecture demonstrates that platform fragmentation can be overcome through aggregation proxies without requiring costly direct API partnerships.

## F. AI Career Chatbot

The chatbot is implemented as a floating widget available on all authenticated pages, powered by GPT-class LLM APIs with HuggingFace Inference API fallback for cost management. A carefully engineered system prompt defines the chatbot as a specialized career guidance assistant and injects live user context: current profile completion status, active application states, ATS score results, and identified skill gaps from the Resume Analyzer.

The chatbot targets three primary use cases: general career guidance (resume writing strategy, job search tactics, professional development planning), platform navigation assistance (applying for roles, interpreting ATS scores, understanding XAI explanations), and mock interview preparation (generating role-specific technical and behavioral interview questions, evaluating answer quality, and providing structured feedback). Conversation history is maintained client-side in Redux state and included in each API request to preserve multi-turn context.

# V. Implementation

## A. Development Environment

Development was conducted on Windows 11 workstations using Visual Studio Code with ESLint and Prettier for code quality enforcement. Node.js v20 LTS served the backend; Python 3.11 with virtual environment isolation served the AI microservice. Docker containerized the Python microservice during integration testing to validate container behavior consistent with production deployment. Git with GitHub provided version control following a structured branching strategy: feature branches merged via pull requests with mandatory peer review.

## B. Database Design

MongoDB Atlas schema comprises five primary collections reflecting the core domain model. Users employs a polymorphic design with role-discriminated embedded subdocuments: job seekers embed resume content and skill arrays directly, while employers embed company references and subscription metadata. Jobs distinguishes internal (employer-posted) from external (JSearch-origin) records through a source field with different required fields per type. Companies are referenced by both Jobs and User employer profiles. Applications maintain full lifecycle state through explicit status transitions (Applied → Reviewed → Shortlisted → Interviewed → Offered/Rejected) with timestamps and employer notes. Sessions maintain a JWT blacklist for cryptographically secure logout without synchronous token revocation infrastructure.

## C. External Integrations

JSearch API via RapidAPI serves as the unified aggregation layer for external job listings. The backend proxy normalizes response schemas through a transformer function mapping JSearch field names to HireAI's internal job schema, ensuring frontend components receive consistent data structures regardless of listing origin. LinkedIn and Google OAuth 2.0 enable single-sign-on authentication and optional professional profile import. Cloudinary provides cloud file storage for resumes and company logos with automatic format conversion and global CDN delivery, ensuring fast resume loading times for employers across geographic locations.

***[📷 IMAGE******: Employer Dashboard Screenshot — show the candidate management interface with ranking list, ATS scores, and the bias audit chart]***

*Fig. 6. Employer dashboard: fairness-aware candidate ranking with demographic parity annotations and bias audit summary.*

# VI. Testing and Results

## A. Testing Strategy

Testing followed a three-tier strategy: unit testing of individual modules, integration testing of cross-component API interactions, and User Acceptance Testing with real end users in structured task scenarios. Unit tests used Jest with Supertest for Node.js backend components, achieving 80% code coverage on controllers and authentication middleware. The Python AI microservice used pytest with fixture-based test data covering normal inputs, edge cases (empty resumes, malformed PDFs), and adversarial inputs (resumes with injection attempts).

## B. User Acceptance Testing

UAT involved 10 job seeker participants (Bahria University engineering students in their final year of study) and 3 employer participants (HR managers from Pakistani technology companies). Participants completed structured task lists without researcher guidance, simulating real-world independent usage. A post-task questionnaire measured comprehension, efficiency, and satisfaction on 5-point Likert scales.

Key findings: 9 of 10 job seekers rated the ATS score and improvement suggestions as clear and actionable (4.2/5.0 mean rating). 8 of 10 found the XAI explanation helpful in understanding match score rationale, meeting the 80% comprehension target. All 3 employer participants reported that AI-assisted candidate ranking was significantly more time-efficient than their current manual screening processes. Common improvement requests identified for future development: clearer visual labeling of the Internal/External mode toggle, additional filter criteria on the employer candidate list, and a more prominent chatbot entry point on mobile viewports.

## C. Performance Results

System performance was evaluated under realistic load conditions simulating concurrent user sessions. All critical latency targets were met or exceeded:

| **Metric** | **Target** | **Achieved** | **Status** |

*Fig. 7. AI module performance: achieved results against defined targets across all **six evaluation** metrics.*

# VII. Discussion

The empirical results validate HireAI's core architectural hypotheses. The 87.3% semantic matching accuracy, achieved through ESCO taxonomy integration and transformer-based embeddings, confirms that vocabulary mismatch — one of the most longstanding limitations of keyword-based recruitment systems — is tractable with currently available NLP infrastructure. The 13-percentage-point improvement over the 74% baseline of pure TF-IDF matching (measured on the same test set) quantifies the practical value of semantic approaches.

The 0.83 demographic parity ratio demonstrates that post-processing fairness-aware ranking achieves meaningful bias mitigation without requiring protected attribute collection or retraining the core matching model. The approach's modularity — fairness correction applied only at ranking, not during semantic matching — preserves the accuracy of the Semantic Intelligence Engine while satisfying the Four-Fifths Rule threshold.

The 80% XAI comprehension rate from UAT is both a validation and a design learning. On one hand, it confirms that counterfactual explanations — framed as specific skill acquisition recommendations with projected score impact — successfully translate technical matching logic into guidance that non-technical users understand and find actionable. On the other hand, the 20% who did not fully comprehend the explanations represent an important design challenge. Post-task interviews with these participants suggest the primary barrier was unfamiliarity with ESCO skill taxonomy terminology, not the explanation format itself.

The Hybrid Job Discovery Engine's architecture demonstrates a generalizable pattern for platform integration in fragmented domains: normalized proxy aggregation allows unified user experience without requiring direct partnership agreements with incumbent platforms. The legal structure of redirecting external applications to original posting URLs while displaying normalized listing data appears consistent with terms-of-service analysis of current API providers.

Zero-cost deployment via free-tier infrastructure (MongoDB Atlas M0, Render.com free tier, Cloudinary free tier) demonstrates that research-grade AI systems can reach production deployment without capital investment. However, free-tier resource constraints — particularly Render.com's cold-start latency for inactive services — represent a production quality limitation that would require paid infrastructure at scale.

# VIII. Limitations and Future Work

Several limitations bound the current system's applicability and performance. ESCO taxonomy coverage is optimized for European and Western labor markets; Pakistani-specific skill terminology, particularly for informal sector occupations, artisanal trades, and local industry roles, has substantially lower coverage. The embedding-based fallback provides partial mitigation, but systematic evaluation of local market coverage gaps remains a priority for future work.

The Fairness-Aware Ranking module infers protected attribute proxies rather than collecting self-reported demographic data, introducing potential measurement inaccuracy. Proxy inference from name analysis or language patterns is known to produce errors, particularly for South Asian names that may not map cleanly to ethnicity or gender categories. Future work should explore privacy-preserving demographic feedback mechanisms that enable more accurate fairness measurement without requiring sensitive data disclosure.

UAT sample size (10 job seekers, 3 employers) is sufficient for formative evaluation but insufficient for statistical generalization of comprehension and satisfaction findings. A larger-scale longitudinal evaluation with representative samples across age groups, educational backgrounds, and industry sectors would strengthen the evidence base.

Planned future development includes: AI video mock interview modules with real-time speech analysis and feedback; salary prediction using aggregated market data from external compensation APIs; e-learning integration with Coursera and Udemy for direct skill gap remediation pathways; Pakistani-specific taxonomy expansion covering local industry sectors; multi-language support including Urdu with right-to-left UI adaptation; employer verified badge system for credential authentication; predictive hiring analytics for employer workforce planning; and native iOS and Android mobile applications.

# IX. Conclusion

HireAI delivers a production-ready, research-validated AI-powered recruitment platform that directly addresses three systemic failures of conventional job portals: vocabulary mismatch in resume-job matching, algorithmic bias in candidate ranking, and opaque rejection without actionable feedback. Through the integration of ESCO-based semantic skill matching, counterfactual XAI, IBM AIF360 fairness-aware ranking, NLP resume analysis, and hybrid job discovery, the system substantially advances the state of practice in recruitment technology.

All six defined success criteria are met within a zero-cost deployment architecture, demonstrating that research-grade AI governance — encompassing explainability, fairness, and transparency — can be operationalized in user-facing production applications without prohibitive infrastructure investment. The evaluation results, validated through structured UAT with real users, confirm measurable improvements in matching accuracy, demographic parity, and user comprehension of AI-generated decisions.

HireAI contributes to UN Sustainable Development Goals 4 (Quality Education), 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth), 9 (Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure), and 10 (Reduced Inequalities) by improving equitable access to employment opportunities and supporting continuous professional skill development through structured, actionable AI feedback. The platform's open architecture and zero-cost deployment model position it as a replicable template for AI-governed recruitment systems in resource-constrained markets.

The authors also acknowledge Bahria University Karachi for providing the academic environment and resources that made this research possible.
